certifications.name: Google Associate Cloud Engineer (ACE)
certifications.description: Validates your ability to deploy applications, monitor operations, manage enterprise solutions, and configure networks and security on GCP.

cheatsheet.summary: Essential practical blueprint for setting up, configuring, deploying, monitoring, and securing GCP environments. Focuses on core infrastructure, container hosting, network topology, and security policies.

coreConcepts.name: IAM Hierarchy & Bindings
coreConcepts.desc: Organization -> Folder -> Project -> Resource. Access is granted via bindings: Member + Role (Primitive, Predefined, Custom) = Access. Principles prioritize least privilege and group-based assignments.

coreConcepts.name: VPC Global Network
coreConcepts.desc: VPCs are global resources. Subnets within them are regional. Internal VMs can communicate across regions natively using private IP addresses over Google's backplane without public internet routing.

coreConcepts.name: Managed Instance Groups (MIGs)
coreConcepts.desc: Utilizes instance templates to auto-scale VM instances based on CPU/traffic load, perform auto-healing via HTTP health checks, and support rolling updates with zero downtime.

coreConcepts.name: Cloud Storage Classes
coreConcepts.desc: Standard (frequent access), Nearline (accessed < once/30 days, backup storage), Coldline (accessed < once/90 days, archive), Archive (accessed < once/365 days, regulatory compliance/DR).

coreConcepts.name: Serverless & Compute Options
coreConcepts.desc: Compute Engine (IaaS VMs), GKE (hybrid/Kubernetes orchestration), App Engine (PaaS runtime web hosting), Cloud Run (serverless containerized app hosting), Cloud Functions (FaaS event-driven).

coreConcepts.name: GKE Pods & Services
coreConcepts.desc: Pods are smallest deployable units. Services define network access: ClusterIP (internal), NodePort (exposes on VM ports), LoadBalancer (provisions External Load Balancer), Ingress (Layer-7 routing).

coreConcepts.name: Cloud SQL vs Cloud Spanner
coreConcepts.desc: Cloud SQL represents regional relational databases (up to 64TB, supports MySQL/Postgres/SQL Server). Cloud Spanner provides globally scale-out relational transactions with strong consistency.

coreConcepts.name: Operations Suite (Stackdriver)
coreConcepts.desc: Cloud Logging (aggregates system logs, routes logs via Log Sinks to BigQuery, GCS, or Pub/Sub), Cloud Monitoring (metrics, dashboards, and triggers alerting policies).

commands.desc: Configures and locks the active command-line context to the specified project ID.
Provisions a virtual machine instance in Compute Engine with specified configurations.
Initializes a managed Kubernetes GKE cluster with N nodes per zone.
Creates a Cloud Storage bucket using the specified class and geographic region location.
Runs an analytical query using standard SQL compiler inside BigQuery datasets.

architecturalPatterns.scenario: Safe Internet Egress for Private VMs
architecturalPatterns.solution: Deploy VMs in a private subnet (no external IPs). Route outgoing traffic (e.g. for software updates) through a Cloud Router and Cloud NAT gateway.
Store static website media in a Cloud Storage bucket with uniform access. Front the bucket with a Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer and enable Cloud CDN for low-latency caching.
Frontend instances write task payloads to a Pub/Sub queue. An asynchronous backend worker running on Cloud Run consumes the events and writes output to Firestore.

flashcards.front: Setting up a cloud solution environment
flashcards.back:
- Create and configure GCP projects and billing accounts
- Manage IAM resource bindings and user groups
- Configure Cloud Logging and Monitoring workspaces
- Install and initialize the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud CLI)

flashcards.front: Planning and configuring a cloud solution
flashcards.back:
- Estimate resource costs using the GCP Pricing Calculator
- Select VM configurations (vCPUs, RAM, local vs persistent disks)
- Plan and configure regional VPC networks and subnets
- Choose database solutions (SQL vs Spanner vs Firestore) based on load

flashcards.front: Deploying and implementing a cloud solution
flashcards.back:
- Deploy Compute Engine VMs and Managed Instance Groups (MIGs)
- Deploy GKE clusters, configure pods, and expose load-balanced services
- Deploy App Engine and Cloud Run serverless applications
- Configure VPC firewall rules and target network tags

flashcards.front: Ensuring successful operation of a cloud solution
flashcards.back:
- Manage Compute Engine VM lifecycles and disk snapshots
- Perform GKE cluster upgrades and configure node autoscaling
- Establish Cloud Storage lifecycle rules and uniform bucket-level permissions
- Monitor system performance metrics and set up alerts

- Apply the Principle of Least Privilege using predefined IAM roles
- Create and assign Service Accounts to VM instances securely
- Inspect audit trails using Cloud Logging filters
- Configure VPC firewall rules and private subnet routes

study_flashcards.front: What is the difference between Compute Engine and App Engine?
study_flashcards.back:
- Compute Engine = IaaS (you manage VMs, OS, patches)
- App Engine = PaaS (Google manages infrastructure)
- App Engine Standard: auto-scales to zero, limited runtimes
- App Engine Flexible: runs custom Docker containers, minimum 1 instance

- Fully managed serverless container platform
- Scales to zero (no charge when idle)
- Supports any language/binary via containers
- Max request timeout: 60 minutes
- Pay per 100ms of CPU/memory used

study_flashcards.front: What is a Managed Instance Group (MIG)?
study_flashcards.back:
- Group of identical VMs created from an instance template
- Supports autoscaling based on CPU, HTTP load, or custom metrics
- Auto-healing via health checks
- Rolling updates and canary deployments
- Regional MIGs span multiple zones for HA

- Both are up to 60-91% cheaper than regular VMs
- Preemptible: max 24-hour lifetime, can be reclaimed anytime
- Spot VMs: same pricing, no max lifetime limit
- Both ideal for batch processing, fault-tolerant workloads
- No SLA, no live migration

study_flashcards.front: Cloud Storage classes and minimum storage durations?
study_flashcards.back:
- Standard: No minimum duration, frequent access
- Nearline: 30-day minimum, accessed < once/month
- Coldline: 90-day minimum, accessed < once/quarter
- Archive: 365-day minimum, accessed < once/year
- All classes have the same API and millisecond access time

study_flashcards.front: Cloud SQL vs Cloud Spanner vs Firestore?
study_flashcards.back:
- Cloud SQL: Regional relational DB (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server), max 64TB, vertical scaling
- Cloud Spanner: Global relational DB with horizontal scaling, strong consistency, 99.999% SLA
- Firestore: Serverless NoSQL document DB, real-time sync, offline support
- Use Cloud SQL for traditional apps, Spanner for global scale, Firestore for mobile/web

study_flashcards.front: What is a VPC and how are subnets scoped?
study_flashcards.back:
- VPC = Virtual Private Cloud, a global resource
- Subnets are regional (span all zones in a region)
- VMs in different regions of same VPC communicate via internal IPs
- Default VPC created with auto-mode subnets in each region
- Custom mode VPCs give full control over IP ranges

- Priority range: 0 (highest) to 65535 (lowest)
- Default rules: deny all ingress (priority 65535), allow all egress (priority 65535)
- Implied rules cannot be deleted
- Rules can target instances by service account or network tag
- Firewall rules are stateful (return traffic auto-allowed)

study_flashcards.front: What are the three types of IAM roles?
study_flashcards.back:
- Basic (Primitive): Owner, Editor, Viewer - broad permissions, avoid in production
- Predefined: Fine-grained roles for specific services (e.g., roles/storage.objectViewer)
- Custom: User-defined roles with specific permissions you select
- Best practice: Use predefined roles, apply least privilege

study_flashcards.front: What is a Service Account and how is it used?
study_flashcards.back:
- An identity for applications/VMs (not humans)
- Has an email format: SA_NAME@PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com
- Attach to VMs/Cloud Run to grant API access
- Can generate keys (avoid when possible, use attached SAs instead)
- Default SA has Editor role - too broad, create custom SAs

study_flashcards.front: IAM resource hierarchy and policy inheritance?
study_flashcards.back:
- Hierarchy: Organization → Folder → Project → Resource
- Policies are inherited downward (union of all policies)
- Cannot restrict permissions granted at a higher level
- Organization policies can enforce constraints across all projects
- Projects are the fundamental billing and resource boundary

study_flashcards.front: GKE cluster types: Zonal vs Regional?
study_flashcards.back:
- Zonal cluster: Single control plane in one zone, nodes in same zone
- Multi-zonal: Single control plane, nodes across multiple zones
- Regional: Control plane replicated across 3 zones, nodes across zones
- Regional clusters provide HA for both control plane and workloads
- Autopilot mode: Google manages nodes, pay per pod

study_flashcards.front: GKE Service types for exposing workloads?
study_flashcards.back:
- ClusterIP: Internal only (default)
- NodePort: Exposes on each node's IP at a static port (30000-32767)
- LoadBalancer: Provisions an external Network Load Balancer
- Ingress: Layer 7 HTTP(S) load balancer with URL-based routing
- Use Ingress for production web apps, LoadBalancer for TCP/UDP

study_flashcards.front: Cloud Logging: Log sinks and export destinations?
study_flashcards.back:
- Log sinks filter and route logs to destinations
- Destinations: Cloud Storage (archival), BigQuery (analysis), Pub/Sub (streaming), Log Buckets
- Inclusion filters select which logs to export
- Exclusion filters prevent specific logs from being ingested
- Admin Activity logs are always on, cannot be disabled

- Condition: What metric threshold triggers the alert
- Notification channel: Email, SMS, Slack, PagerDuty, webhook
- Documentation: Runbook/notes attached to incident
- Uptime checks: HTTP(S), TCP, or ICMP probes from global locations
- Custom metrics can be created via API or OpenTelemetry

study_flashcards.front: Essential gcloud commands for the ACE exam?
study_flashcards.back:
- gcloud config set project PROJECT_ID (set active project)
- gcloud compute instances create NAME --zone=ZONE
- gcloud container clusters get-credentials CLUSTER
- gcloud app deploy (deploy App Engine app)
- gcloud run deploy --image=IMAGE --platform=managed
- gsutil mb gs://BUCKET (create Cloud Storage bucket)

- Billing account linked to one or more projects
- Budgets send alerts at thresholds (50%, 90%, 100%)
- Budgets do NOT stop spending by default
- Use billing export to BigQuery for cost analysis
- Committed Use Discounts (CUDs): 1-3 year for up to 57% off
- Sustained Use Discounts (SUDs): Auto-applied for consistent usage

study_flashcards.front: Cloud NAT vs Cloud VPN vs Interconnect?
study_flashcards.back:
- Cloud NAT: Lets private VMs access internet without external IPs (egress only)
- Cloud VPN: Encrypted tunnel over public internet to on-prem (up to 3 Gbps per tunnel)
- Dedicated Interconnect: Physical connection, 10/100 Gbps, 99.99% SLA
- Partner Interconnect: Via service provider, 50 Mbps-50 Gbps
- Use Interconnect for high bandwidth, VPN for quick setup

- Deployment Manager: GCP-native IaC, uses YAML/Jinja2/Python templates
- Terraform: Multi-cloud IaC by HashiCorp, uses HCL language
- Both support preview/plan before applying changes
- Deployment Manager is free, tightly integrated with GCP
- Terraform is the industry standard, supports 100+ providers

- pd-standard: HDD-backed, lowest cost, sequential I/O
- pd-balanced: SSD-backed, cost-effective, general purpose
- pd-ssd: SSD-backed, highest IOPS for random access
- pd-extreme: Highest performance, configurable IOPS (up to 120K)
- Regional PDs: Replicated across 2 zones for HA
- Local SSD: Physically attached, ephemeral, highest throughput

questions.question: [IAM Roles Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Your development team needs to store and retrieve files from a Cloud Storage bucket. The team should be able to view, list, delete, and overwrite objects, but must not be allowed to change bucket permissions. Which IAM role should you grant?
questions.explanation: Storage Object Admin allows full control over Cloud Storage objects (files), but does not allow modifying bucket-level IAM policies or permissions. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-associate-cloud-engineer-q1)

questions.question: [Compute Engine Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to set up autoscaling for a containerized web application running on Compute Engine VMs. What resource type must you create first to enable autoscaling?
questions.explanation: Compute Engine autoscaling is a feature of Managed Instance Groups (MIGs). You cannot configure autoscaling on unmanaged instance groups. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-associate-cloud-engineer-q2)

questions.question: [Cloud Storage Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Which Cloud Storage class is most cost-effective for data that you plan to access less than once a year?
questions.explanation: Archive Storage is the lowest-cost, highly durable storage service for data archiving, online backup, and disaster recovery. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-associate-cloud-engineer-q3)

questions.question: [VPC Networking Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You create a new custom VPC network. Within this network, you create two subnets in different regions. What is the default internal connectivity status between VMs in these subnets?
options: They cannot communicate internally because they are in different regions.
They can communicate internally by default because Google Cloud custom VPCs have system-generated routing rules that connect all subnets in the VPC.
They can only communicate internally if you set up VPC Network Peering.
They can only communicate if they are assigned public external IP addresses.
questions.explanation: In Google Cloud, VPC networks are global. Subnets within the same VPC, regardless of their regions, are automatically connected via system-generated internal routes. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-associate-cloud-engineer-q4)

questions.question: [Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to deploy a containerized application to GKE and ensure that Google automatically manages the node upgrading, scaling, and provisioning. Which GKE cluster mode should you select?
questions.explanation: In Autopilot mode, GKE provisions and manages the cluster's underlying infrastructure, including nodes, scaling, security, and upgrades. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-associate-cloud-engineer-q5)

questions.question: [VPC Networking Scenario 8] In your company's Project H environment: You create a new custom VPC network. Within this network, you create two subnets in different regions. What is the default internal connectivity status between VMs in these subnets?
options: They cannot communicate internally because they are in different regions.
They can communicate internally by default because Google Cloud custom VPCs have system-generated routing rules that connect all subnets in the VPC.
They can only communicate internally if you set up VPC Network Peering.
They can only communicate if they are assigned public external IP addresses.
questions.explanation: In Google Cloud, VPC networks are global. Subnets within the same VPC, regardless of their regions, are automatically connected via system-generated internal routes. (Difficulty: Hard, Question Ref: google-associate-cloud-engineer-q39)

questions.question: [Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Scenario 8] In your company's Project H environment: You want to deploy a containerized application to GKE and ensure that Google automatically manages the node upgrading, scaling, and provisioning. Which GKE cluster mode should you select?
questions.explanation: In Autopilot mode, GKE provisions and manages the cluster's underlying infrastructure, including nodes, scaling, security, and upgrades. (Difficulty: Hard, Question Ref: google-associate-cloud-engineer-q40)

questions.question: [IAM Roles Scenario 9] In your company's Project I environment: Your development team needs to store and retrieve files from a Cloud Storage bucket. The team should be able to view, list, delete, and overwrite objects, but must not be allowed to change bucket permissions. Which IAM role should you grant?
questions.explanation: Storage Object Admin allows full control over Cloud Storage objects (files), but does not allow modifying bucket-level IAM policies or permissions. (Difficulty: Hard, Question Ref: google-associate-cloud-engineer-q41)

questions.question: [Compute Engine Scenario 9] In your company's Project I environment: You want to set up autoscaling for a containerized web application running on Compute Engine VMs. What resource type must you create first to enable autoscaling?
questions.explanation: Compute Engine autoscaling is a feature of Managed Instance Groups (MIGs). You cannot configure autoscaling on unmanaged instance groups. (Difficulty: Hard, Question Ref: google-associate-cloud-engineer-q42)

certifications.name: Google Cloud Digital Leader
certifications.description: Validates foundational knowledge of cloud concepts, Google Cloud products, services, tools, features, and primary use cases.

cheatsheet.summary: Business-focused blueprint validating foundational cloud concepts, shared responsibility principles, Google Cloud resource hierarchies, and business cases for database, AI, and analytic migrations.

coreConcepts.name: CapEx vs OpEx Shift
coreConcepts.desc: Moving from Capital Expenditure (upfront investments in physical servers, power, real estate) to Operational Expenditure (pay-as-you-go elastic utility model matching demand).

coreConcepts.name: IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS
coreConcepts.desc: Infrastructure (Compute Engine, Disks where you manage OS), Platform (App Engine, Cloud Run where Google manages OS/runtimes), Software (Google Workspace/packaged web apps).

coreConcepts.name: Shared Responsibility Model
coreConcepts.desc: Google manages security 'of' the cloud (physical centers, network fibers, hardware virtualizations). The customer manages security 'in' the cloud (IAM controls, database schemas, firewall configurations).

coreConcepts.name: GCP Resource Hierarchy
coreConcepts.desc: Enforces structure: Organization -> Folders -> Projects -> Resources. Inherits permissions down the hierarchy, enabling automated billing and compliance constraints.

coreConcepts.name: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)
coreConcepts.desc: Calculated by comparing cloud operational fees against baseline on-premises expenses (cooling, electricity, physical space, hardware refreshes, network maintenance staffs).

coreConcepts.name: Big Data and AI Offerings
coreConcepts.desc: BigQuery (serverless data warehouse for analytical SQL), Looker (business intelligence visualization dashboards), Vertex AI (unified AI development platform).

coreConcepts.name: Cloud Storage & Databases
coreConcepts.desc: Cloud Storage (object storage), Cloud SQL (managed SQL), Cloud Spanner (global scale relational), Firestore (NoSQL document).

commands.desc: Interactive web utility used to estimate monthly infrastructure pricing based on expected usage patterns.
Configure budget targets and email triggers in Billing Console to prevent unexpected cloud invoice spikes.

architecturalPatterns.solution: Migrate on-premises Windows/Linux servers directly to Compute Engine VMs (lift-and-shift) to eliminate datacenter maintenance.
Load sales transaction feeds into BigQuery, aggregate metrics using SQL, and build interactive dashboards using Looker for business stakeholders.

flashcards.back:
- Shift from Capital Expenditure (CapEx) to Operational Expenditure (OpEx)
- Understand cloud types: Public, Private, Hybrid, and Multi-cloud
- Define database modernization, serverless benefits, and API strategies
- Calculate Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) and ROI business value models

flashcards.front: Innovating with Data in the Cloud
flashcards.back:
- Classify unstructured vs structured vs semi-structured data
- Select storage categories: Object, File, Block, Relational, and NoSQL
- Understand analytics: Data lakes, data warehouses, and streaming pipelines
- Identify business use cases for BigQuery, Looker, and Pub/Sub

- Match compute platforms (VMs, Containers, Serverless) to workloads
- Understand serverless scaling benefits and cold start concepts
- Evaluate hybrid network pathways (Cloud VPN vs Interconnect)
- Plan migration paths: Lift-and-shift, re-platforming, and refactoring

- Differentiate between security of the cloud vs in the cloud (Shared Responsibility)
- Enforce access controls using the Cloud IAM hierarchy
- Enforce network defenses: firewalls, DDoS protection, and WAF
- Define SRE principles, SLAs, SLOs, and logging metrics

study_flashcards.front: CapEx vs OpEx in cloud computing?
study_flashcards.back:
- CapEx (Capital Expenditure): Upfront investment in physical infrastructure
- OpEx (Operational Expenditure): Pay-as-you-go cloud spending
- Cloud shifts IT from CapEx to OpEx model
- Benefits: No upfront cost, scale on demand, pay only for what you use
- Reduces financial risk of overprovisioning

study_flashcards.front: Public vs Private vs Hybrid vs Multi-cloud?
study_flashcards.back:
- Public cloud: Resources shared across tenants (GCP, AWS, Azure)
- Private cloud: Dedicated infrastructure for one organization
- Hybrid cloud: Mix of on-premises and public cloud
- Multi-cloud: Using multiple public cloud providers
- Anthos enables hybrid/multi-cloud Kubernetes management

study_flashcards.front: What is Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)?
study_flashcards.back:
- Full cost of owning and operating IT infrastructure
- Includes: Hardware, software, personnel, power, cooling, facility
- Cloud TCO eliminates facility/hardware costs
- Google TCO calculator helps compare on-prem vs cloud
- Hidden on-prem costs: Maintenance, upgrades, downtime

study_flashcards.front: BigQuery: What is it and key features?
study_flashcards.back:
- Serverless, petabyte-scale data warehouse
- Uses SQL for querying structured and semi-structured data
- Columnar storage with automatic optimization
- Supports ML models directly via BigQuery ML
- Pricing: On-demand ($5/TB scanned) or flat-rate (slots)
- Separation of storage and compute

study_flashcards.front: Structured vs Unstructured vs Semi-structured data?
study_flashcards.back:
- Structured: Organized in rows/columns (SQL databases, spreadsheets)
- Unstructured: No predefined format (images, videos, PDFs, audio)
- Semi-structured: Has tags/markers but no rigid schema (JSON, XML, Avro)
- Cloud Storage: Best for unstructured data
- BigQuery/Cloud SQL: Best for structured data

- Data lake: Raw data in any format, schema-on-read (Cloud Storage)
- Data warehouse: Processed, structured data, schema-on-write (BigQuery)
- Data lakes store everything, warehouses store curated analytics data
- Lakehouse: Combines both approaches (BigLake)
- Data lakes are cheaper for storage, warehouses are faster for queries

- Compute Engine: Full VMs (IaaS) - most control
- GKE: Managed Kubernetes - container orchestration
- App Engine: PaaS - just deploy code
- Cloud Run: Serverless containers - scales to zero
- Cloud Functions: FaaS - event-driven, single-purpose
- More managed = less control but less operational burden

- Regions: Independent geographic areas (e.g., us-central1)
- Zones: Isolated locations within regions (e.g., us-central1-a)
- 40+ regions, 121+ zones worldwide
- Multi-region: Services spanning multiple regions (e.g., Cloud Storage)
- Edge locations: Google's CDN points of presence (PoPs)

- Google secures: Physical infrastructure, hardware, network, encryption at rest
- Customer secures: Data, access policies, application security, IAM configuration
- Shared: OS patches (depends on service), network config
- More managed services = more Google responsibility
- IaaS: Customer manages most; SaaS: Google manages most

study_flashcards.front: Google Cloud encryption at rest and in transit?
study_flashcards.back:
- All data encrypted at rest by default (AES-256)
- All data encrypted in transit between Google data centers
- HTTPS enforced for all GCP APIs
- Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK): You control keys in Cloud KMS
- Customer-supplied encryption keys (CSEK): You provide your own keys

- Rehost (Lift & Shift): Move as-is to cloud VMs
- Replatform: Minor optimizations (e.g., managed DB)
- Refactor: Rewrite for cloud-native architecture
- Repurchase: Switch to SaaS solution
- Retire: Decommission unused systems
- Retain: Keep on-premises (not ready to migrate)

study_flashcards.front: Pre-trained AI APIs in Google Cloud?
study_flashcards.back:
- Vision AI: Image classification, OCR, face detection
- Natural Language AI: Sentiment analysis, entity recognition
- Speech-to-Text / Text-to-Speech: Audio transcription
- Translation AI: 100+ languages
- Video AI: Object tracking, shot detection
- No ML expertise needed to use these APIs

- SLA (Service Level Agreement): Contract with penalties if not met
- SLO (Service Level Objective): Internal reliability target
- SLI (Service Level Indicator): Actual measured metric (e.g., latency)
- Example: SLI = 99.95% uptime, SLO = 99.9%, SLA = 99.5%
- SLOs should be stricter than SLAs to catch issues early

- Carbon neutral since 2007
- Goal: Run on 24/7 carbon-free energy by 2030
- Carbon Footprint tool shows emissions per project
- Region Picker considers carbon impact
- Cloud is 1.5-3x more energy efficient than on-prem data centers

- Right-sizing recommendations from Recommender
- Committed Use Discounts (CUDs): 1-3 year commit for savings
- Preemptible/Spot VMs for fault-tolerant workloads
- Cloud Storage lifecycle rules to auto-transition classes
- Budget alerts and billing export to BigQuery
- Autoscaling to match demand

questions.question: [Cloud Concepts Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: What is the primary benefit of shifting from Capital Expenditure (CapEx) to Operational Expenditure (OpEx) in cloud computing?
options: Paying a fixed amount regardless of usage
Paying only for resources consumed, reducing upfront hardware costs
Ensuring that servers are physically hosted on-premises
Eliminating the need to configure software security
questions.explanation: OpEx allows businesses to pay as they go for operational resources rather than investing heavily upfront in physical infrastructure (CapEx). (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-cloud-digital-leader-q1)

questions.question: [Compute & Serverless Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Which Google Cloud service lets you run containerized applications without provisioning or managing virtual machines?
questions.explanation: Cloud Run is a fully managed serverless compute platform that runs containerized applications. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-cloud-digital-leader-q2)

questions.question: [Shared Responsibility Model Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: According to the Google Cloud Shared Responsibility Model, who is responsible for the physical security of data centers?
options: Shared equally between Google Cloud and the customer
questions.explanation: Under the Google Cloud Shared Responsibility Model, Google Cloud is solely responsible for physical security of data centers. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-cloud-digital-leader-q3)

questions.question: [Database Selection Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Which Google Cloud database service is best suited for storing transactional relational data that requires global scalability and strong consistency?
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner is a fully managed, mission-critical, relational database service that provides transactional consistency at global scale. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-cloud-digital-leader-q4)

questions.question: [Data Analytics Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Your company needs a serverless data warehouse to perform SQL analysis on petabytes of structured data with minimal setup. Which service should you choose?
questions.explanation: BigQuery is Google Cloud's fully managed, serverless enterprise data warehouse designed for SQL queries on large-scale datasets. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-cloud-digital-leader-q5)

questions.explanation: Under the Google Cloud Shared Responsibility Model, Google Cloud is solely responsible for physical security of data centers. (Difficulty: Hard, Question Ref: google-cloud-digital-leader-q38)

questions.question: [Database Selection Scenario 8] In your company's Project H environment: Which Google Cloud database service is best suited for storing transactional relational data that requires global scalability and strong consistency?
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner is a fully managed, mission-critical, relational database service that provides transactional consistency at global scale. (Difficulty: Hard, Question Ref: google-cloud-digital-leader-q39)

questions.question: [Data Analytics Scenario 8] In your company's Project H environment: Your company needs a serverless data warehouse to perform SQL analysis on petabytes of structured data with minimal setup. Which service should you choose?
questions.explanation: BigQuery is Google Cloud's fully managed, serverless enterprise data warehouse designed for SQL queries on large-scale datasets. (Difficulty: Hard, Question Ref: google-cloud-digital-leader-q40)

questions.question: [Cloud Concepts Scenario 9] In your company's Project I environment: What is the primary benefit of shifting from Capital Expenditure (CapEx) to Operational Expenditure (OpEx) in cloud computing?
options: Paying a fixed amount regardless of usage
Paying only for resources consumed, reducing upfront hardware costs
Ensuring that servers are physically hosted on-premises
Eliminating the need to configure software security
questions.explanation: OpEx allows businesses to pay as they go for operational resources rather than investing heavily upfront in physical infrastructure (CapEx). (Difficulty: Hard, Question Ref: google-cloud-digital-leader-q41)

questions.question: [Compute & Serverless Scenario 9] In your company's Project I environment: Which Google Cloud service lets you run containerized applications without provisioning or managing virtual machines?
questions.explanation: Cloud Run is a fully managed serverless compute platform that runs containerized applications. (Difficulty: Hard, Question Ref: google-cloud-digital-leader-q42)

certifications.name: Google Cloud Generative AI Leader
certifications.description: Validates foundational knowledge of Generative AI concepts, responsible AI development, and deploying gen AI solutions strategically using Vertex AI, Gemini, and Agent Builder.

cheatsheet.summary: Strategic overview of Generative AI capabilities, prompt engineering strategies, RAG architecture, responsible AI evaluations, and Vertex AI enterprise application builder tools.

coreConcepts.name: Generative vs Discriminative AI
coreConcepts.desc: Generative AI produces new data instances (text, images, audio, code) by learning underlying patterns, whereas Discriminative AI classifies inputs or predicts labels.

coreConcepts.name: Foundation Models & LLMs
coreConcepts.desc: Massive deep learning neural networks (like Gemini) pre-trained on diverse datasets, capable of reasoning, translation, and adaptation to custom tasks.

coreConcepts.name: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
coreConcepts.desc: Architectural design that queries external databases or document stores to retrieve context, appending it to the prompt to ground model outputs in factual, real-time data.

coreConcepts.name: Responsible AI & Ethics
coreConcepts.desc: Frameworks for safety auditing, fairness, transparency, and bias reduction under Google Cloud's AI Principles. Employs safety filters to block harmful inputs/outputs.

coreConcepts.name: Prompt Engineering vs Tuning
coreConcepts.desc: Prompt Engineering designs context within input queries. Tuning (e.g. Fine-Tuning, Parameter-Efficient Tuning) updates actual model weights using training data.

coreConcepts.name: Vertex AI Agent Builder
coreConcepts.desc: No-code/low-code platform that lets enterprises build search engines and conversational chatbots grounded in internal PDFs, websites, or database sources.

coreConcepts.name: Gemini for Google Cloud
coreConcepts.desc: Always-on AI collaborator providing code suggestions, log analysis, configuration troubleshooting, and billing guidance inside the GCP console.

commands.desc: Interactive console for prototyping prompts, configuring temperature parameters, and comparing response outputs.
Bind custom agent instances to secure Google Cloud Storage or BigQuery data stores containing internal wikis.

architecturalPatterns.solution: Create a chat agent using Vertex AI Agent Builder, link it to customer documentation stored in GCS, and embed it as a grounded web assistant.
Establish a search engine grounded in internal financial records inside a VPC Service Controls perimeter. Configure safety thresholds to block sensitive outputs.

flashcards.back:
- Differentiate Generative AI from Discriminative AI models
- Understand Large Language Models (LLMs) and Foundation Models
- Define model parameters: tokens, context windows, and temperatures
- Identify the causes of hallucinations and mitigation methods

- Assess business workflows suitable for Gen AI integrations
- Identify common use cases: summarization, search, copywriting, code helpers
- Structure ROI calculations for enterprise AI projects
- Build build-vs-buy decisions for foundational LLMs

- Deploy Vertex AI Studio for rapid prompt design and prototyping
- Ground models using Vertex AI Agent Builder (Search & Conversation)
- Utilize Model Garden for open-source model evaluations
- Understand Gemini for Google Cloud operations workflows

- Structure prompt engineering techniques (Few-Shot, Chain-of-Thought)
- Design Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) using external databases
- Secure vector database mappings and embeddings
- Optimize response accuracy with real-time grounding sources

- Apply Google's Responsible AI Principles in software designs
- Configure safety filters and toxicity thresholds on LLM APIs
- Manage copyright protections and corporate data governance policies
- Monitor model drift, bias, and output safety indicators over time

study_flashcards.back:
- Generative AI: Creates new content (text, images, code, audio)
- Discriminative AI: Classifies/predicts from existing data
- Gen AI models learn patterns from training data to generate novel outputs
- Examples: GPT, Gemini (generative) vs spam filters, classifiers (discriminative)
- Foundation models are large generative models trained on broad datasets

study_flashcards.front: What are tokens and context windows?
study_flashcards.back:
- Tokens: Basic units of text (words, subwords, characters) processed by LLMs
- 1 token ≈ 4 characters or ¾ of a word in English
- Context window: Max tokens the model can process in one request
- Gemini 1.5 Pro: Up to 2 million tokens context window
- Larger context = more information but higher cost and latency

study_flashcards.front: What is temperature in LLM inference?
study_flashcards.back:
- Controls randomness/creativity of model outputs
- Temperature 0: Deterministic, most probable response
- Temperature 1: More creative, diverse responses
- Temperature > 1: Very random, potentially incoherent
- Low temp for factual tasks, high temp for creative writing

study_flashcards.front: What are hallucinations in Gen AI?
study_flashcards.back:
- Model generates plausible but factually incorrect information
- Causes: Training data gaps, pattern extrapolation, lack of grounding
- Mitigation: RAG (grounding with external data), temperature tuning
- Fact-checking, human-in-the-loop review
- Grounding with Google Search or enterprise data reduces hallucinations

study_flashcards.front: What is Vertex AI and its key components?
study_flashcards.back:
- Google Cloud's unified ML/AI development platform
- Vertex AI Studio: Prompt design, tuning, and testing UI
- Model Garden: Access to 100+ foundation and open-source models
- Vertex AI Agent Builder: Build AI agents with search and conversation
- Vertex AI Pipelines: MLOps orchestration for production ML

study_flashcards.front: Vertex AI Model Garden vs Vertex AI Studio?
study_flashcards.back:
- Model Garden: Catalog of pre-trained models (Gemini, PaLM, Llama, Mistral)
- Deploy, fine-tune, or evaluate models from the garden
- Vertex AI Studio: Interactive UI for prompt engineering
- Test prompts, adjust parameters, compare model outputs
- Studio is for prototyping; Garden is for model selection

- Zero-shot: Direct instruction with no examples
- Few-shot: Provide examples of desired input/output pairs
- Chain-of-Thought (CoT): Ask model to show reasoning steps
- System prompts: Set model persona, constraints, and format
- Best practice: Be specific, provide context, iterate on prompts

- Combines LLM generation with external knowledge retrieval
- Steps: Query → Retrieve relevant docs → Augment prompt → Generate answer
- Reduces hallucinations by grounding responses in real data
- Uses vector databases and embeddings for semantic search
- Better than fine-tuning for rapidly changing information

study_flashcards.front: What are embeddings and vector databases?
study_flashcards.back:
- Embeddings: Dense numerical representations of text/images/data
- Capture semantic meaning (similar concepts are close in vector space)
- Vector databases: Specialized stores for similarity search (Vertex AI Vector Search)
- Used in RAG to find relevant documents for a query
- Dimensionality typically 256-1536 for text embeddings

study_flashcards.front: Common Gen AI enterprise use cases?
study_flashcards.back:
- Customer service: AI chatbots, email response drafting
- Content creation: Marketing copy, product descriptions
- Code generation: Code completion, bug fixing, documentation
- Search: Enterprise knowledge base with natural language queries
- Summarization: Meeting notes, document summaries, report generation

study_flashcards.front: Build vs Buy decisions for Gen AI?
study_flashcards.back:
- Buy (API): Use Gemini API for standard tasks, fastest time-to-value
- Customize: Fine-tune a foundation model on your data
- Build: Train from scratch (requires massive data and compute)
- Most enterprises should start with API + RAG
- Build only when unique domain data gives competitive advantage

- Be socially beneficial
- Avoid creating or reinforcing unfair bias
- Be built and tested for safety
- Be accountable to people
- Incorporate privacy design principles
- Uphold high standards of scientific excellence
- Google will NOT develop AI for: weapons, surveillance violating norms, harm

- Safety filters block harmful content (violence, hate speech, PII)
- Configurable thresholds: Block None, Block Few, Block Some, Block Most
- Content moderation classifiers score output toxicity
- Data governance: Control what training data is used
- Audit logging tracks all model invocations and outputs

- Gemini Ultra/Pro/Flash: Different performance vs cost tradeoffs
- Natively multimodal: Text, images, audio, video, code
- Gemini Flash: Fastest, lowest cost, best for high-volume tasks
- Gemini Pro: Balanced performance for complex reasoning
- Available via Vertex AI API and Google AI Studio

study_flashcards.front: Fine-tuning vs Prompt engineering vs RAG?
study_flashcards.back:
- Prompt engineering: Cheapest, fastest, no training needed
- RAG: Adds external knowledge at inference time, no retraining
- Fine-tuning: Adapts model weights with custom data, expensive
- Prompt engineering first → RAG if knowledge needed → Fine-tune if style/format critical
- Fine-tuning changes the model; RAG changes the context

questions.question: [Responsible AI Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Which of the following aligns with Google's Responsible AI principles when deploying generative models?
options: Maximizing the model's creativity regardless of factual accuracy
Ensuring safety evaluations, monitoring bias, and implementing safety filters
Locking model outputs to prevent any variation in response
Sharing user prompts publicly to foster model collaboration
questions.explanation: Responsible AI practices require continuous evaluation for safety, bias tracking, and deploying guardrails/filters to prevent unsafe content generation. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-generative-ai-leader-q1)

questions.question: [Vertex AI Offerings Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Your business needs to build a custom AI chatbot that can answer customer queries using your internal company wikis and PDF files, without writing complex machine learning pipelines. Which Google Cloud tool should you select?
options: Compute Engine VMs with manually installed PyTorch
Vertex AI Agent Builder (Search and Conversation)
BigQuery ML with custom linear regression models
questions.explanation: Vertex AI Agent Builder enables rapid construction of enterprise-grade search engines and generative chatbots grounded in custom internal data with minimal code. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-generative-ai-leader-q2)

questions.question: [Generative AI Concepts Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: What is the primary difference between Generative AI and Discriminative AI?
options: Generative AI only processes numerical data, whereas Discriminative AI processes natural text.
Generative AI creates new data instances based on learned patterns, while Discriminative AI classifies existing data.
Generative AI runs only on-premises, while Discriminative AI runs in the cloud.
Discriminative AI uses deep learning, whereas Generative AI does not.
questions.explanation: Generative AI models learn data distributions to generate new, original content (e.g., text, images), whereas Discriminative AI predicts labels or categories by distinguishing between data points. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-generative-ai-leader-q3)

questions.question: [Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Your company's LLM frequently outputs outdated or incorrect information about product availability. Which design pattern should you implement to ground the model's responses in real-time inventory databases?
options: Increasing the model's temperature parameter to maximum
Fine-tuning the foundation model every hour
Hardcoding answers inside the prompt itself
questions.explanation: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) queries dynamic external databases to retrieve factual, real-time context and appends it to the user's prompt, reducing hallucinations. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-generative-ai-leader-q4)

questions.question: [Gemini for Google Cloud Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Which tool provides conversational assistance to help write infrastructure code, analyze cloud costs, and troubleshoot GCP resource configuration?
questions.explanation: Gemini for Google Cloud acts as an always-on AI collaborator that assists with writing code, explaining cloud resource properties, and optimizing GCP billing/security. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-generative-ai-leader-q5)

certifications.name: Google Professional Cloud Architect (PCA)
certifications.description: Evaluates your ability to design, develop, and manage robust, secure, scalable, and highly available GCP solutions.

cheatsheet.summary: Advanced design blueprint for enterprise environments. Focuses on multi-region high availability, compliance guardrails, disaster recovery, global load balancing, and secure hybrid cloud connectivity.

coreConcepts.name: HA & Disaster Recovery (DR)
coreConcepts.desc: Active-Active configurations distribute request loads. Active-Passive uses failover routes. RTO (recovery speed) and RPO (data loss threshold) dictate storage replication choices.

coreConcepts.name: GCP Global Load Balancing
coreConcepts.desc: Global External HTTP(S) LB (Layer-7 routing, SSL offloading, Cloud Armor, and CDN integration). Network Load Balancers (Layer-4 TCP/UDP, high throughput).

coreConcepts.name: VPC Service Controls Perimeters
coreConcepts.desc: Establishes a security perimeter around multi-tenant APIs (like Cloud Storage or BigQuery) to prevent malicious or accidental data exfiltration.

coreConcepts.name: Enterprise Hybrid Interconnect
coreConcepts.desc: Dedicated Interconnect (direct physical links to Google cores, 10G/100G, highest SLAs). Partner Interconnect (private links via ISPs). HA VPN (99.99% SLA, IPsec over public web).

coreConcepts.name: Governance & Organization Policies
coreConcepts.desc: Define organizational-level rules (e.g., block external IPs on VMs, restrict locations to US/Europe, enforce Shared VPC structures across folders).

coreConcepts.name: Databases at Scale
coreConcepts.desc: Cloud Spanner (fully managed, relational, synchronous multi-region replication, horizontal scale). Bigtable (low-latency NoSQL time-series). BigQuery (petabyte-scale serverless analytics).

commands.desc: Initiates configuration of a physical direct fiber connection to Google Cloud network edge.
Designates the specified project as the host project to share subnets across tenant service projects.
Applies system-wide organization policies to block insecure defaults (e.g. IAM service account key creations).

architecturalPatterns.solution: Front-end traffic routed via Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer to MIGs in US and Europe. Data tier deployed on a multi-region Cloud Spanner database for global consistency.
Ingest database backups from on-premises over Dedicated Interconnect. Process logs in a Project enclosed in a VPC Service Controls perimeter, storing outputs in BigQuery.
Set up an organizational log sink to export all system and access logs into a central bucket configured with Cloud Storage Object Retention Lock.

flashcards.back:
- Map business objectives to highly available, multi-region technical targets
- Design global load balancing and DNS routing configurations
- Architect robust disaster recovery strategies (minimizing RTO/RPO)
- Select database and storage solutions matching capacity plans

- Enforce centralized IAM controls and resource organizational rules
- Define network perimeters using VPC Service Controls
- Design data encryption strategies (managed CMEK vs supplying CSEK keys)
- Align systems to regulations (HIPAA, GDPR, PCI-DSS) via logging

- Implement autoscaling rules for Compute Engine MIGs and GKE
- Configure multi-region active-active failover mechanisms
- Design caching strategies using Cloud Memorystore (Redis)
- Establish decoupled event pipelines (Pub/Sub + Dataflow)

- Design hybrid network pathways (HA VPN vs Dedicated Interconnect)
- Implement VPC network configurations: subnetting, Peering, Shared VPC
- Optimize latency using Cloud CDN edge caches
- Enforce firewall rules and private access configurations

study_flashcards.front: Designing for high availability on GCP?
study_flashcards.back:
- Use regional Managed Instance Groups across zones
- Regional GKE clusters for container workloads
- Cloud Spanner or multi-region Cloud Storage for data
- Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer for traffic distribution
- Design for failure: assume any component can fail

- Monolithic: Single deployable unit, simpler but harder to scale
- Microservices: Independent services communicating via APIs
- GCP tools: Cloud Run, GKE for microservices deployment
- Pub/Sub for async communication between services
- Each service can scale independently and use different languages

- Shared VPC: Central network admin, projects share a host VPC
- VPC Peering: Connect two separate VPCs, non-transitive
- Shared VPC: Best for organizations with centralized networking
- VPC Peering: Best for connecting independent projects
- Shared VPC supports IAM control; Peering is simpler setup

study_flashcards.front: Defense in depth strategy on GCP?
study_flashcards.back:
- Network: VPC firewall rules, Cloud Armor DDoS protection
- Identity: IAM, Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP), MFA
- Data: Encryption at rest (default), CMEK, DLP API
- Application: Web Security Scanner, Binary Authorization
- Operations: Cloud Audit Logs, Security Command Center
- Multiple overlapping layers of security controls

- Migrate for Compute Engine: VM migrations from on-prem/other clouds
- Migrate for Anthos: Modernize VMs to containers
- Database Migration Service: MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server
- Transfer Service: Large-scale data transfer to Cloud Storage
- BigQuery Data Transfer Service: SaaS data into BigQuery

study_flashcards.front: When to use Cloud Bigtable vs BigQuery?
study_flashcards.back:
- Bigtable: Low-latency NoSQL for high-throughput read/write (IoT, time-series)
- BigQuery: Analytics warehouse for complex SQL queries on large datasets
- Bigtable: Single-digit ms latency, row-key based access
- BigQuery: Seconds-minutes latency, columnar storage
- Bigtable for operational data; BigQuery for analytical data

- Cold: Backup data only, longest RTO (hours-days)
- Warm: Scaled-down environment ready to scale up, moderate RTO
- Hot: Fully running duplicate environment, shortest RTO (minutes)
- Dual-region: Active-active across regions for near-zero RTO
- RPO determined by backup/replication frequency

- Right-size VMs using Recommender
- Use CUDs for predictable workloads (up to 57% off)
- Spot VMs for fault-tolerant batch processing
- Autoscaling to match demand
- Storage lifecycle policies to transition to cheaper tiers
- Export billing to BigQuery for analysis

- Resource Location Restriction org policy: Enforce data residency
- VPC Service Controls: Prevent data exfiltration
- Access Transparency: Logs of Google admin access to your data
- Assured Workloads: Pre-configured compliance environments
- Supports HIPAA, PCI-DSS, SOC 2, ISO 27001, FedRAMP

- Pub/Sub: Async messaging between services (at-least-once delivery)
- Eventarc: Route events from GCP services to targets
- Cloud Functions: Respond to events (HTTP, Pub/Sub, Cloud Storage)
- Cloud Tasks: Managed task queues with rate limiting
- Workflows: Orchestrate multi-service processes

- Full lifecycle API management platform
- API gateway, analytics, monetization, developer portal
- Rate limiting, quotas, OAuth/API key security
- API versioning and traffic management
- Use for external-facing APIs with partner/developer access

- Manage GKE clusters on-prem, AWS, Azure from single control plane
- Anthos Config Management: GitOps-based policy enforcement
- Anthos Service Mesh: Observability and security for microservices
- Migrate for Anthos: Convert VMs to containers
- Consistent Kubernetes experience across environments

- Cloud Identity: IDaaS for managing users and groups
- Workforce Identity Federation: External IdP users access GCP
- Workload Identity Federation: External workloads access GCP (no service account keys)
- Google Cloud Directory Sync: Sync from Active Directory/LDAP
- SSO with SAML 2.0 or OIDC

- Global HTTP(S) LB: Layer 7, URL-based routing, SSL termination
- Regional TCP/UDP LB (Network LB): Layer 4, pass-through
- Internal TCP/UDP LB: Private load balancing within VPC
- SSL Proxy LB: Global Layer 4 for SSL traffic
- TCP Proxy LB: Global Layer 4 for non-SSL TCP traffic

- Fully managed in-memory data store
- Redis: Rich data structures, persistence, pub/sub, replication
- Memcached: Simple key-value caching, multi-threaded
- Use cases: Session caching, leaderboards, rate limiting
- Sub-millisecond latency for read-heavy workloads

questions.question: [Designing for High Availability Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You are designing a high-availability multi-tier application. The database layer must support failover with zero data loss across regions. Which architecture should you choose?
options: Cloud SQL with cross-region read replicas promoted manually
Cloud Spanner with a multi-region configuration
Compute Engine VM instances with database replication over Cloud VPN
Cloud SQL with High Availability (HA) enabled in a single region
questions.explanation: Cloud Spanner multi-region configurations provide synchronous replication and database failover with a 99.999% availability SLA and zero data loss. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-architect-q1)

questions.question: [Cloud Migration Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You are migrating 100 TB of historical log data from your on-premises SAN to Cloud Storage. The migration must complete within 2 days over a 1 Gbps dedicated internet link. What is the recommended strategy?
options: Upload data using gsutil multi-threading over the internet.
Order a Google Transfer Appliance, load the data on-premises, and ship it to Google.
Configure a Cloud VPN connection and copy the files.
Use Partner Interconnect to transfer the data.
questions.explanation: A 1 Gbps connection would take around 10-12 days to upload 100 TB. The Google Transfer Appliance is the recommended, secure offline data transfer option for this scale and timeframe. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-architect-q2)

questions.question: [Security and Compliance Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Your enterprise organization needs to prevent data exfiltration from Cloud Storage buckets and BigQuery datasets, ensuring they are only accessible from within your private network or authorized subnets. What should you configure?
questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls allows you to define a security perimeter around Google Cloud resources to prevent data exfiltration and block unauthorized access. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-architect-q3)

questions.question: [Load Balancing Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You need to distribute global HTTPS traffic to backends located in multiple regions (US, Europe, Asia) to minimize latency for end-users. Which load balancer should you use?
options: Global External HTTP(S) Load Balancer (Classic or Modern)
questions.explanation: The Global External HTTP(S) Load Balancer is a proxy load balancer that routes user requests to the closest backend instance based on geography and capacity. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-architect-q4)

questions.question: [Disaster Recovery Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to configure disaster recovery for VM instances running in us-east1. In the event of a zone outage, the VMs should be bootable in us-east4 with minimal RTO. What is the most cost-effective recovery method?
options: Maintain active duplicate VMs running in us-east4.
Configure schedule-based persistent disk snapshots and replicate them to us-east4.
Export VM disks to Cloud Storage and import them when needed.
Use GKE multi-cluster ingress to duplicate VM instances.
questions.explanation: Replicating persistent disk snapshots across regions provides a low-cost, secure backup of VM states that can be quickly restored as boot disks in another region. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-architect-q5)

questions.explanation: A 1 Gbps connection would take around 10-12 days to upload 100 TB. The Google Transfer Appliance is the recommended, secure offline data transfer option for this scale and timeframe. (Difficulty: Medium, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-architect-q17)

certifications.name: Google Professional Data Engineer (PDE)
certifications.description: Assesses skills in designing, building, operationalizing, and securing data processing systems on Google Cloud.

cheatsheet.summary: Data architecture blueprint validating pipeline pipelines, streaming analytics (Dataflow/PubSub), serverless warehouses (BigQuery), time-series databases (Bigtable), and compliance orchestrations.

coreConcepts.name: BigQuery Partitioning & Clustering
coreConcepts.desc: Partitioning splits tables by date or integer range (limits scanned bytes). Clustering sorts data by key columns inside partitions (speeds up grouping/filtering).

coreConcepts.name: Cloud Bigtable Row Key Design
coreConcepts.desc: NoSQL wide-column time-series store. Avoid sequential keys (e.g. timestamp first) to prevent hotspotting. Design row keys as sensor_id#timestamp to group queries.

coreConcepts.name: Dataflow Windowing & Stream
coreConcepts.desc: Based on Apache Beam. Tumbling (fixed non-overlapping), Hopping (overlapping), Session (inactivity gap). Watermarks track event-time vs processing-time progression.

coreConcepts.name: Composer Workflow Orchestration
coreConcepts.desc: Fully managed Apache Airflow. Runs Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) in Python to automate ETL pipeline steps, coordinate Dataproc Spark jobs, and trigger BigQuery updates.

coreConcepts.name: Data Security & DLP masking
coreConcepts.desc: Utilize Cloud Data Loss Prevention (DLP) API to scan, identify, and redact PII data (e.g., credit card numbers, SSNs) inside streaming pipelines before database writes.

commands.desc: Creates a structured date-partitioned BigQuery table with resource expiration policies.
Executes analytical SQL commands against BigQuery datasets using standard SQL engine.
Deploys a managed Apache Spark/Hadoop processing cluster on compute nodes.

architecturalPatterns.solution: Stream web events to Pub/Sub, compute metrics using sliding windows in Dataflow, and stream directly into BigQuery for visualization.
Ingest millisecond sensor data into Pub/Sub, parse and filter metrics in Dataflow, and write outputs to Bigtable using a row key structure of 'sensor_id#timestamp'.
Upload files to a staging Cloud Storage bucket. Trigger a Dataflow job that calls the DLP API to mask PII data, and load the anonymized records into BigQuery.

flashcards.back:
- Choose storage solutions (BigQuery, Bigtable, Cloud Storage)
- Design pipeline schemas and structures (batch vs stream)
- Plan infrastructure scaling and workflow pipelines
- Configure data archiving and retention policies

- Ingest data streams using Pub/Sub event brokers
- Code data transformations inside Cloud Dataflow (Apache Beam)
- Orchestrate daily data workflows using Cloud Composer (Airflow)
- Migrate legacy Hadoop/Spark jobs to Cloud Dataproc

- Query data warehouses using BigQuery SQL commands
- Train machine learning models directly in SQL via BigQuery ML (BQML)
- Deploy custom ML models on Vertex AI endpoints
- Anonymize and redact patient data using Cloud DLP API scans

- Monitor pipelines for bottlenecks using Cloud Monitoring
- Test data outputs for accuracy and schema conformance
- Manage data access controls with column-level IAM tags
- Troubleshoot workflow step failures and retry pipelines

study_flashcards.back:
- Dataflow: Fully managed, Apache Beam-based, serverless
- Dataproc: Managed Hadoop/Spark clusters, configurable
- Dataflow: Best for new pipelines, auto-scaling, streaming
- Dataproc: Best for existing Hadoop/Spark workloads migration
- Dataflow handles windowing and watermarks natively

- Global, real-time messaging service
- At-least-once delivery (pull or push subscriptions)
- Message retention: Up to 31 days
- Ordering with ordering keys
- Dead letter topics for failed messages
- Exactly-once processing with Dataflow integration

- Fixed windows: Non-overlapping, fixed duration (e.g., every 5 min)
- Sliding windows: Overlapping, defined by duration + period
- Session windows: Based on activity gaps per key
- Global window: Single window for all data (default)
- Watermarks track event-time progress for late data handling

- Partitioning: Divides table by date/integer range, reduces scan cost
- Clustering: Sorts data within partitions by up to 4 columns
- Partition pruning: Only scans relevant partitions
- Clustering improves filter and aggregate query performance
- Best practice: Partition by date, cluster by frequently filtered columns

- On-demand: $5/TB of data scanned (first 1 TB/month free)
- Capacity (editions): Pay for dedicated compute slots
- Storage: $0.02/GB/month (active), $0.01/GB (long-term after 90 days)
- Streaming inserts: $0.01/200 MB
- Use partitioning and clustering to reduce scan costs

- Linear/logistic regression
- K-means clustering
- Matrix factorization (recommendations)
- Time series (ARIMA_PLUS)
- Deep Neural Networks (TensorFlow)
- XGBoost and random forest
- Train directly with SQL, no data export needed

study_flashcards.front: Data governance and lineage on GCP?
study_flashcards.back:
- Dataplex: Unified data governance and management
- Data Catalog: Metadata management and discovery
- Data Lineage: Track data flow across systems
- DLP API: Discover and protect sensitive data (PII)
- Column-level security in BigQuery for fine-grained access

- Ingest: Pub/Sub (async) or Dataflow (direct)
- Process: Dataflow (Apache Beam) for transforms and windowing
- Store: BigQuery (streaming buffer) or Bigtable (low-latency)
- Analyze: BigQuery SQL or Looker dashboards
- Pub/Sub → Dataflow → BigQuery is the canonical streaming pipeline

- Data Fusion: Visual ETL pipeline builder (CDAP-based), no-code
- Dataflow: Code-based (Apache Beam), fully serverless
- Data Fusion: Best for business users and visual pipeline design
- Dataflow: Best for complex custom transformations
- Data Fusion generates Dataproc jobs under the hood

- Design row keys to avoid hotspotting
- Don't start row keys with timestamps (causes sequential writes)
- Use reversed timestamps or salted keys for even distribution
- Store related data in the same column family
- Tall and narrow tables preferred over short and wide
- Use row key prefixes for efficient range scans

- BigQuery Data Transfer Service: Scheduled transfers from SaaS
- Storage Transfer Service: Move from S3, Azure, or on-prem
- gsutil/gcloud: CLI-based transfer to Cloud Storage then load
- Dataflow: Transform during migration
- Federation: Query external data without moving it

- Dataset-level, table-level, column-level access control
- Row-level security with row access policies
- Data masking with policy tags
- Authorized views: Share query results without exposing source tables
- CMEK for customer-managed encryption keys
- VPC Service Controls to prevent data exfiltration

- Managed Apache Airflow for workflow orchestration
- DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs) define task dependencies
- Schedule and monitor complex multi-service data pipelines
- Integrates with BigQuery, Dataflow, Dataproc, GCS
- Use when pipelines have complex dependencies and scheduling needs

- Looker: Enterprise BI platform with LookML semantic layer
- Looker Studio: Free self-service dashboarding tool
- Looker: Governed metrics, data modeling, embedded analytics
- Looker Studio: Quick visualizations, connects to 800+ sources
- Use Looker for enterprise; Looker Studio for ad-hoc reporting

- Classic templates: Pre-built, Google-provided pipelines
- Flex templates: Custom pipelines packaged as Docker containers
- Templates allow non-developers to run pipelines
- Flex templates support dynamic parameters and custom environments
- Classic templates: Limited customization but faster to deploy

questions.question: [BigQuery Optimization Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You run heavy analytical queries in BigQuery that filter by a transaction date and group by a store ID. How should you design the table to minimize costs and optimize query speed?
options: Partition by transaction date and cluster by store ID.
Cluster by transaction date and partition by store ID.
Create separate tables for every store ID.
Increase the reservation slots in BigQuery.
questions.explanation: Partitioning by date narrows down the scanned data blocks (reducing cost), while clustering by store ID sorts data within partitions to speed up grouping and filtering. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-data-engineer-q1)

questions.question: [Bigtable Schema Design Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You are designing a row key for a Cloud Bigtable database storing IoT sensor metrics. The query pattern is always retrieving records for a specific sensor ID over a recent time range. How should you format the row key to avoid hotspotting?
questions.explanation: Starting the row key with the sensor ID groups that sensor's metrics together. Appending the timestamp allows time-range queries. Placing timestamp first would cause hotspotting on the node handling the current time. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-data-engineer-q2)

questions.question: [Data Pipeline Orchestration Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You need to orchestrate a complex workflow that extracts data from Cloud Storage, triggers a Dataproc Spark job, and loads results into BigQuery daily. Which Google Cloud service should you use?
questions.explanation: Cloud Composer is a fully managed workflow orchestration service built on Apache Airflow, ideal for authoring, scheduling, and monitoring pipelines. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-data-engineer-q3)

questions.question: [Stream Processing Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You are building a real-time data streaming pipeline that needs to ingest data from mobile apps, perform aggregations over a sliding window, and handle late-arriving data. Which service combination should you select?
options: Pub/Sub for ingestion and Cloud Dataflow for processing
Pub/Sub for ingestion and BigQuery for processing
Cloud Storage for ingestion and Dataproc for processing
Cloud SQL for ingestion and Cloud Composer for processing
questions.explanation: Pub/Sub handles scalable ingestion, while Cloud Dataflow (based on Apache Beam) supports streaming windowing, watermarks, and triggers for late data. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-data-engineer-q4)

questions.question: [Database Selection Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Your application requires a NoSQL document database that supports real-time synchronization, mobile offline access, and ACID transactions. Which service should you choose?
questions.explanation: Firestore is a serverless NoSQL document database designed for mobile and web apps, supporting ACID transactions and offline sync. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-data-engineer-q5)

questions.question: [BigQuery Optimization Scenario 2] In your company's Project B environment: You run heavy analytical queries in BigQuery that filter by a transaction date and group by a store ID. How should you design the table to minimize costs and optimize query speed?
options: Partition by transaction date and cluster by store ID.
Cluster by transaction date and partition by store ID.
Create separate tables for every store ID.
Increase the reservation slots in BigQuery.
questions.explanation: Partitioning by date narrows down the scanned data blocks (reducing cost), while clustering by store ID sorts data within partitions to speed up grouping and filtering. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-data-engineer-q6)

questions.question: [Bigtable Schema Design Scenario 2] In your company's Project B environment: You are designing a row key for a Cloud Bigtable database storing IoT sensor metrics. The query pattern is always retrieving records for a specific sensor ID over a recent time range. How should you format the row key to avoid hotspotting?
questions.explanation: Starting the row key with the sensor ID groups that sensor's metrics together. Appending the timestamp allows time-range queries. Placing timestamp first would cause hotspotting on the node handling the current time. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-data-engineer-q7)

questions.explanation: Cloud Composer is a fully managed workflow orchestration service built on Apache Airflow, ideal for authoring, scheduling, and monitoring pipelines. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-data-engineer-q8)

certifications.name: Google Professional Cloud Developer (PCD)
certifications.description: Tests your proficiency in designing, building, testing, and deploying cloud-native applications on Google Cloud.

cheatsheet.summary: Development blueprint focusing on serverless app design (Cloud Run/Functions), API authentication, CI/CD orchestration, distributed tracing, profiling, and secure configuration management.

coreConcepts.name: Serverless Scaling & Runtimes
coreConcepts.desc: Cloud Run runs containerized microservices, scaling down to zero when idle. Set minimum instances to prevent cold starts. Adjust concurrency settings to optimize throughput per instance.

coreConcepts.name: CI/CD & Release Management
coreConcepts.desc: Automated pipelines: Cloud Build compiles code, Artifact Registry stores images securely, and Cloud Deploy coordinates canary or blue-green releases to GKE or Cloud Run.

coreConcepts.name: Distributed Trace & Monitoring
coreConcepts.desc: Cloud Trace logs HTTP request latencies across microservice boundaries. Cloud Profiler monitors CPU and memory usage in code. Cloud Logging captures console outputs.

coreConcepts.name: API Gateways & Security
coreConcepts.desc: Expose microservices using API Gateway or Apigee for request throttling, API key verification, OAuth authentication, and cataloging REST resources.

coreConcepts.name: Secrets Management
coreConcepts.desc: Store sensitive keys (API tokens, passwords) in Secret Manager. Grant the compute service account Secret Manager Viewer access, and inject secrets as env variables at startup.

commands.desc: Triggers a build on Cloud Build and substitutes variables dynamically.
Reads the raw payload of the latest active secret version from the command-line.
Deploys service to Cloud Run with customized scaling and concurrency configurations.

architecturalPatterns.solution: Deploy a Node.js microservice to Cloud Run. Cache frequent database query outputs in Cloud Memorystore (Redis) to decrease response latencies.
Submit transactions to API Gateway. Write records to Pub/Sub to decouple services, and process transactions asynchronously via a worker container on Cloud Run.

flashcards.back:
- Build applications using serverless runtimes (Cloud Run, Functions)
- Secure APIs with API Gateway and authentication tokens
- Configure containerized microservices in GKE clusters
- Manage session state using Cloud Memorystore (Redis) caching

- Author build steps in Cloud Build (cloudbuild.yaml)
- Store container images in Artifact Registry
- Deploy applications with Cloud Deploy canary rollouts
- Inject credentials securely via Secret Manager

- Trace request latencies across services using Cloud Trace
- Profile code resource usage with Cloud Profiler
- Review application logs in Cloud Logging
- Set up error reporting alerts for runtime exceptions

study_flashcards.front: What is Cloud Run and how does it scale?
study_flashcards.back:
- Fully managed serverless container platform
- Scales automatically from 0 to thousands of instances based on traffic
- Can configure min-instances to avoid cold starts
- Supports any language, runtime, or binary via container images
- Pay-per-use: billed only when container processes requests (100ms granularity)

study_flashcards.front: What is Cloud Build and how do you configure it?
study_flashcards.back:
- Serverless CI/CD platform that executes builds on Google infrastructure
- Configured via a `cloudbuild.yaml` or `cloudbuild.json` file
- Consists of sequential build steps, each running in a Docker container
- Integrates with GitHub/Bitbucket to trigger builds on code pushes
- Can build, test, and deploy containerized/non-containerized applications

- Artifact Registry is the evolution of Container Registry (deprecating GCR)
- Supports Docker container images AND language packages (npm, pip, maven, NuGet)
- Supports regional repositories and fine-grained IAM permissions
- Integrated vulnerability scanning for container images
- Repository URL format: REGION-docker.pkg.dev/PROJECT-ID/REPO-NAME

study_flashcards.front: How do you manage secrets securely in Google Cloud?
study_flashcards.back:
- Use Secret Manager to store API keys, passwords, credentials, and other credentials
- Secrets are versioned and encrypted at rest
- Grant permissions using IAM role `roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor` to service accounts
- Inject secrets into Cloud Run/Functions as environment variables or mount as volumes
- Avoid storing secrets in source code, configuration files, or container images

study_flashcards.front: Cloud Trace vs Cloud Profiler vs Cloud Debugger?
study_flashcards.back:
- Cloud Trace: Distributed tracing system that tracks latency across microservices
- Cloud Profiler: Continuous profiling of CPU/memory usage inside application code to optimize performance
- Cloud Debugger: Inspect application state without stopping the app (deprecated, replaced by OpenTelemetry/third-party alternatives)
- Use Trace to find bottlenecks, Profiler to optimize resource utilization

study_flashcards.front: How does Error Reporting help developers?
study_flashcards.back:
- Automatically aggregates crash details and stack traces from running services
- Group similar errors together and sends alerts on new exceptions
- Supports major languages (Python, Go, Node.js, Java, etc.)
- Link errors directly to source code repositories or bug trackers
- Displays occurrence rates and affected user metrics

study_flashcards.front: Cloud Pub/Sub push vs pull subscriptions?
study_flashcards.back:
- Pull subscription: Consumers request messages from Pub/Sub (ideal for batch/large volume)
- Push subscription: Pub/Sub delivers messages to a webhook HTTPS endpoint (ideal for serverless/low latency)
- Push subscriptions require registering a verified domain or using service account tokens for authentication
- Messages are retained up to 7 days by default if unacknowledged

study_flashcards.front: Cloud Tasks: What is it and when to use?
study_flashcards.back:
- Asynchronous task execution service with rate-limiting and scheduling
- Use to offload long-running background tasks from HTTP requests
- Supports task de-duplication, retries, and scheduled execution times
- Targets HTTP endpoints (e.g., Cloud Run, Cloud Functions) or App Engine queue handlers
- Bounded queue rates prevent overwhelming downstream services

- Blue-Green: Maintain two identical environments. Deploy to Green, test, then switch traffic 100% (fast rollback)
- Canary: Deploy changes to a small subset of instances (e.g., 5% of traffic), monitor errors, then gradually roll out to 100%
- Cloud Run supports traffic splitting for easy canary testing
- GKE supports canary deployments using Kubernetes Services and multiple Deployments

study_flashcards.front: How do you connect to Cloud SQL securely from microservices?
study_flashcards.back:
- Use Cloud SQL Auth Proxy as a sidecar or background process
- Proxy uses IAM credentials to authenticate and establish a secure TLS tunnel
- Eliminates the need to allowlist client IP addresses or manage SSL certs manually
- Connection string format: `/cloudsql/PROJECT-ID:REGION:INSTANCE-ID`
- Use private IP within a shared VPC for direct, secure routing

- Allows users or service accounts to log into Cloud SQL (PostgreSQL/MySQL) using IAM credentials
- Eliminates hardcoded database usernames and passwords
- Leverages OAuth2 access tokens which are short-lived and automatically rotated
- Simplifies permission management via centralized IAM roles

study_flashcards.front: App Engine Standard vs App Engine Flexible?
study_flashcards.back:
- Standard: Fast startup, scales to zero, sandboxed runtimes (Python, Java, Go, etc.), limited libraries
- Flexible: Runs custom Docker containers, uses VMs, slow startup, minimum 1 instance, no scaling to zero
- Choose Standard for cost-sensitive, standard web applications
- Choose Flexible for custom binaries, background threads, or writing to disk

study_flashcards.front: What is Cloud Functions and when to use?
study_flashcards.back:
- Event-driven serverless FaaS (Function-as-a-Service)
- Executes single-purpose code in response to events (e.g., GCS upload, Pub/Sub message, HTTP request)
- Automatically manages scaling, patching, and OS maintenance
- Billed per 100ms and execution count
- Use for lightweight processing, webhooks, or file conversions

study_flashcards.front: What is Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) for developers?
study_flashcards.back:
- Secures access to applications running on App Engine, Compute Engine, or GKE
- Intercepts requests, validates user identity via Google Accounts, and checks IAM permissions
- Eliminates the need for a corporate VPN to secure internal development/staging tools
- Passes user identity headers (`x-goog-authenticated-user-email`) to the application

- Mitigates data exfiltration risk by blocking service API calls crossing network perimeters
- Can cause application API calls (e.g., Cloud Storage read, BigQuery insert) to fail with a 403 error if not inside the perimeter
- Developers must configure access levels or ingress/egress rules to allow legitimate cross-perimeter traffic
