certifications.name: Google Professional Cloud Security Engineer
certifications.description: Tests your ability to design, develop, and manage a secure infrastructure using Google Cloud security technologies.

cheatsheet.summary: Advanced security blueprint covering key management (KMS), resource perimeters (VPC SC), firewall structures, WAF filtering (Cloud Armor), identity proxies (IAP), and sensitive data management.

coreConcepts.name: Encryption at Rest Portfolio
coreConcepts.desc: GMEK (Google-Managed, default), CMEK (Customer-Managed, key generation/lifecycle in Cloud KMS), CSEK (Customer-Supplied, keys managed on-premises).

coreConcepts.name: VPC Service Controls
coreConcepts.desc: Locks down APIs (like GCS or BigQuery) by enclosing projects in service perimeters, preventing network exfiltrations and restricting traffic to verified sources.

coreConcepts.name: Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP)
coreConcepts.desc: Secures web applications running on App Engine, GKE, or VMs without using VPNs, by verifying user identity and context via IAM policy rules.

coreConcepts.name: Cloud Armor & WAF
coreConcepts.desc: Provides Layer-7 firewall filtering, DDoS mitigation, and OWASP Top 10 web attack protection (e.g. blocking SQL injections) at HTTP(S) Load Balancer level.

coreConcepts.name: IAM Governance & Workload Identity
coreConcepts.desc: Workload Identity allows GKE Pods to authenticate directly as GCP Service Accounts, avoiding the need to download or mount static private service account key files.

commands.desc: Initializes a key vault keyring wrapper at a specific location.
Generates a key within the keyring for data encryption purposes.
Creates a firewall rule to allow SSH access only via Cloud IAP TCP forwarding range.

architecturalPatterns.solution: Expose an internal administrative portal running on Compute Engine VMs behind an HTTPS Load Balancer with Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) enabled, restricting access to verified company users.
Isolate the BigQuery database and raw data storage buckets inside a VPC Service Controls perimeter. Configure an ingress rule to allow access only from an authorized VPC network.
Stream logs to a Pub/Sub topic, execute a Dataflow pipeline that calls the DLP API to mask SSNs and phone numbers, and save the sanitized logs to BigQuery.

flashcards.back:
- Enforce centralized IAM policies using Resource Hierarchy
- Authenticate GKE pods safely with Workload Identity
- Configure Cloud Identity directory syncs and user groups
- Set up Conditional Access and IAP access controls

- Manage encryption keys using Customer-Managed Keys (CMEK) in KMS
- Establish project security perimeters via VPC Service Controls
- Redact sensitive database records using Cloud DLP API scans
- Secure cloud storage objects with retention locks

- Mitigate web attacks (SQL injection) via Cloud Armor WAF rules
- Restrict VM traffic using network tags and Firewall rules
- Set up secure remote management via IAP TCP forwarding
- Enable DDoS protections on HTTP(S) Load Balancers

study_flashcards.back:
- IAM Conditions: Grant access based on attributes (time, resource, IP)
- Context-Aware Access: Access decisions based on user identity AND device security
- BeyondCorp Enterprise: Zero-trust security model
- Access Levels: Define trust criteria (device encryption, OS version)
- Enforced via Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP)

- Create security perimeters around GCP resources
- Prevents data exfiltration from authorized APIs
- Service perimeter: Defines which projects and services are protected
- Access levels and ingress/egress rules for exceptions
- Dry-run mode to test before enforcement

- DDoS protection for HTTP(S) Load Balancer
- WAF rules: OWASP Top 10 protection, SQL injection, XSS
- Adaptive Protection: ML-based attack detection
- Rate limiting and bot management
- Geo-based access control (allow/deny by country)
- Pre-configured rules for common attack patterns

- Discover, classify, and de-identify sensitive data
- 150+ built-in info types (SSN, credit cards, email, etc.)
- De-identification: Masking, tokenization, bucketing
- Scan: Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Datastore
- Risk analysis for quasi-identifiers (k-anonymity, l-diversity)

- Google-managed keys: Default, no configuration needed
- Customer-managed keys (CMEK): You control key rotation/destruction
- Customer-supplied keys (CSEK): You provide keys, Google doesn't store them
- Cloud HSM: Hardware security modules for FIPS 140-2 Level 3
- Cloud EKM: Keys managed in external key management system

- Allow external workloads (AWS, Azure, GitHub Actions) to access GCP
- No service account key management needed
- Uses OIDC or SAML tokens from external IdP
- Maps external identities to GCP IAM principals
- Recommended over downloading service account keys

- Centralized security and risk management dashboard
- Standard tier: Vulnerability scanning, compliance monitoring
- Premium tier: Threat detection, container security, Event Threat Detection
- Security Health Analytics: Misconfiguration detection
- Web Security Scanner: OWASP vulnerability scanning
- Findings: Unified view of security issues across all projects

- Admin Activity: Who did what admin action (always on, free, 400-day retention)
- Data Access: Who accessed data (must enable, charged, 30-day default)
- System Event: Google-initiated actions (always on, free, 400-day)
- Policy Denied: Actions denied by policies (always on, free)
- Export logs via sinks to Cloud Storage, BigQuery, or Pub/Sub

study_flashcards.front: Private Google Access and Private Service Connect?
study_flashcards.back:
- Private Google Access: VMs without external IPs access Google APIs
- Enabled per subnet, routes traffic via internal network
- Private Service Connect: Private endpoint for Google APIs in your VPC
- Creates a consumer endpoint with an internal IP
- Keeps all traffic on Google's private network

- Zero-trust security model: Never trust, always verify
- Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP): Per-request authentication and authorization
- Access Context Manager: Define device and network conditions
- Endpoint Verification: Inventory and assess device compliance
- No VPN needed for secure application access

- Enable Workload Identity for pod-level IAM
- Use Binary Authorization for trusted images only
- Enable Shielded GKE Nodes (Secure Boot, vTPM)
- Network Policies to restrict pod-to-pod traffic
- Private clusters: No public IPs on nodes
- Regular vulnerability scanning with Artifact Analysis

- Secret Manager: Store and access secrets (passwords, API keys, certs)
- Cloud KMS: Manage encryption keys (encrypt/decrypt operations)
- Secret Manager stores the secret value itself
- KMS stores keys that encrypt data (you manage the encrypted data)
- Use both together: Secret Manager encrypted with CMEK

study_flashcards.front: Software supply chain security on GCP?
study_flashcards.back:
- Artifact Registry: Store and scan container images
- Binary Authorization: Only deploy attested images
- Cloud Build: Provenance attestation (SLSA compliance)
- Container Analysis: Vulnerability scanning of images
- SLSA framework: Levels 1-4 of supply chain security maturity

- Centralized constraints across GCP organization
- Resource location restriction: Enforce data residency
- Disable service account key creation
- Enforce uniform bucket-level access
- Restrict VM external IP addresses
- Inherited down the org hierarchy, overridable at lower levels

- Event Threat Detection: Scans logs for threats (crypto mining, malware, IAM anomalies)
- Chronicle: Google's security analytics platform (SIEM/SOAR)
- Chronicle ingests massive log volumes for threat hunting
- Integrates with SCC for unified security management
- Uses Google threat intelligence for detection rules

questions.question: [Data Encryption Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Your company's security policy requires that all data stored in Cloud Storage must be encrypted using keys that you generate, manage, and rotate outside of Google Cloud. Which encryption method should you use?
options: Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK) via Cloud KMS
questions.explanation: CSEKs allow you to supply your own encryption keys. Google uses these keys to encrypt the data, but never stores them on disk. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-security-engineer-q1)

questions.question: [Web Application Security Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Your web application running on Compute Engine VMs is experiencing Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) and SQL injection attacks from the internet. Which service should you configure to protect it?
questions.explanation: Cloud Armor protects web applications from DDoS attacks and OWASP Top 10 risks (like SQL injection and cross-site scripting). (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-security-engineer-q2)

questions.question: [Identity and Access Management Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to grant users access to an administrative VM instance without exposing its public IP address or configuring a VPN. Which security service should you implement?
questions.explanation: IAP's TCP forwarding feature allows you to control who can access administrative services (like SSH or RDP) on VMs without public IP addresses. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-security-engineer-q3)

questions.question: [Sensitive Data Discovery Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to automatically scan Cloud Storage buckets for personally identifiable information (PII) like credit card numbers or social security IDs, and redact them. Which tool should you use?
questions.explanation: Sensitive Data Protection (formerly Cloud DLP) helps you discover, classify, and redact sensitive data in cloud storage and databases. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-security-engineer-q4)

questions.question: [Auditing and Logging Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You need to audit all read actions on files inside your Cloud Storage buckets. By default, these logs are disabled to save storage costs. Which log type must you enable?
questions.explanation: Data Access Audit logs record when API calls read resource configurations or user-provided data (disabled by default, except for BigQuery). (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-security-engineer-q5)

certifications.name: Google Professional Cloud Network Engineer
certifications.description: Validates your expertise in designing, planning, and managing Google Cloud network architectures.

cheatsheet.summary: Advanced network engineering blueprint. Focuses on VPC architectures, Shared VPC design, hybrid routes (Interconnect/VPN), BGP configurations, private endpoint resolutions, and CDN caching.

coreConcepts.name: Shared VPC Architecture
coreConcepts.desc: Shares VPC subnets from a central Host Project to multiple Service Projects, enforcing administrative network segregation while enabling shared compute connectivity.

coreConcepts.name: VPC Network Peering
coreConcepts.desc: Connects two VPC networks internally with low latency and high bandwidth across distinct organizations, without using public gateway hops.

coreConcepts.name: Hybrid Connectivities
coreConcepts.desc: Dedicated Interconnect (direct physical fiber, 10G/100G). Partner Interconnect (private ISP circuit, 50M-10G). HA VPN (99.99% SLA, IPsec over public web, dual tunnels).

coreConcepts.name: Dynamic Routing with BGP
coreConcepts.desc: Uses Cloud Router to exchange routing tables dynamically using Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) over VPN or Interconnect links to on-premises routers.

coreConcepts.name: Private Google Access
coreConcepts.desc: Enables VM instances that only have internal IP addresses in a subnet to reach global Google APIs (like BigQuery, GCS) securely without public internet routes.

commands.desc: Creates a custom-mode VPC network without auto-generated default regional subnets.
Creates a subnet and enables Private Google Access.
Deploys a Cloud Router with a BGP Autonomous System Number (ASN).

architecturalPatterns.solution: Deploy all subnets, firewalls, and Interconnect lines in a Shared VPC host project. Share subnets with application-specific service projects for isolation.
Establish a VPN tunnel to AWS. Configure Cloud DNS forwarding zones in GCP to route requests for '.aws' domains to AWS Route53 resolvers.

flashcards.back:
- Plan custom subnet allocations across multi-region networks
- Configure Shared VPCs to share networks with service projects
- Establish private project connections with VPC Network Peering
- Enable Private Google Access for VMs in internal subnets

- Deploy Dedicated Interconnect direct physical links
- Set up Partner Interconnect routed private circuits
- Configure HA VPN dual tunnels for 99.99% availability
- Exchange network paths dynamically using BGP on Cloud Router

- Deploy Global HTTP(S) Load Balancer for multi-region backends
- Resolve domains across peered VPCs using private Cloud DNS zones
- Cache static files at edge locations with Cloud CDN
- Configure Cloud DNS forwarding zones for hybrid name resolution

study_flashcards.back:
- Auto-mode: Automatically creates subnets in each region (10.128.0.0/9)
- Custom-mode: No auto subnets, you define all CIDR ranges
- Auto-mode: Good for quick setup, limited CIDR control
- Custom-mode: Production recommended, full control
- Can convert auto to custom (one-way, irreversible)

- Capture IP traffic metadata for VPC subnets
- Logged per VM network interface every 5 seconds (configurable)
- Use cases: Network monitoring, forensics, compliance
- Aggregation interval: 5s, 30s, 1min, 5min, 10min, 15min
- Sample rate: 50% default, configurable 0-100%
- Stored in Cloud Logging, exportable to BigQuery

- External: Distributes internet traffic to backends
- Internal: Distributes internal/private traffic within VPC
- External HTTP(S): Global, Layer 7, URL routing
- Internal TCP/UDP: Regional, Layer 4, passthrough
- Internal HTTP(S): Regional, Layer 7, proxy-based
- Cross-region internal LB available with Premium Tier

- Managed authoritative DNS service (100% SLA)
- Public zones: Internet-facing domain resolution
- Private zones: Internal DNS within VPC networks
- DNS forwarding: Route queries to on-prem DNS servers
- DNS peering: Share private zones across VPC networks
- DNSSEC: Cryptographic validation of DNS responses

- Dedicated Interconnect: Physical connection 10/100 Gbps, 99.99% SLA
- Partner Interconnect: Via service provider 50 Mbps-50 Gbps
- Cross-Cloud Interconnect: Direct connection to other clouds
- Requires LOA-CFA and colocation facility for Dedicated
- VLAN attachments connect Interconnect to VPC
- Supports BGP for dynamic routing

study_flashcards.front: Cloud VPN: HA VPN vs Classic VPN?
study_flashcards.back:
- HA VPN: 99.99% SLA, 2 interfaces per gateway, dynamic routing (BGP)
- Classic VPN: 99.9% SLA, single interface, static or dynamic routing
- HA VPN: Recommended for production, redundant tunnels
- Each tunnel supports up to 3 Gbps
- IKEv2 encryption with pre-shared keys or certificates

- Fully managed BGP router for dynamic route exchange
- Learns routes from on-prem and propagates to VPC
- Supports graceful restart for BGP session resilience
- ASN: Private range 64512-65534 for GCP
- Custom route advertisements for selective prefix sharing
- Required for HA VPN and Interconnect

- Applied at organization or folder level
- Override or supplement VPC-level firewall rules
- Actions: Allow, Deny, Go to next (delegate to lower level)
- Priority-based evaluation (lower number = higher priority)
- Enables centralized network security governance
- Target: All VMs or specific service accounts

- Premium Tier (default): Google's global backbone, optimal routing
- Standard Tier: Regular internet routing, regional LBs only
- Premium: Lower latency, higher cost, global LB support
- Standard: Higher latency, lower cost, regional only
- Set per resource (IP address or forwarding rule)

- Clones network traffic from VM instances for analysis
- Mirrors ingress and/or egress traffic
- Send to internal load balancer backed by collector VMs
- Use cases: IDS/IPS, network forensics, compliance monitoring
- Filter by protocol, IP range, or direction
- Does not affect source VM performance

- Content delivery network using Google's edge PoPs
- Works with External HTTP(S) Load Balancer
- Cache modes: USE_ORIGIN_HEADERS, CACHE_ALL_STATIC, FORCE_CACHE_ALL
- Signed URLs and signed cookies for access control
- Cache invalidation API for content updates
- Reduces latency and origin server load

- Hub-and-spoke: Central VPC (hub) connected to project VPCs (spokes)
- Shared VPC: Centralized network administration
- VPC Peering: Direct VPC-to-VPC connectivity (non-transitive)
- Cloud VPN/Interconnect: On-prem to cloud connectivity
- NCC (Network Connectivity Center): Hub for multi-cloud/hybrid networking

- Internal (private) IPs: Assigned from subnet CIDR, free
- External (public) IPs: Ephemeral (free when attached) or static (charged when idle)
- Alias IP ranges: Assign multiple IPs to a single VM NIC
- Private Google Access: Reach Google APIs without external IP
- IPv6: Supported on VPCs and subnets (dual-stack)

- VPC Flow Logs: IP traffic metadata
- Firewall Rules Logging: Track rule matches
- Packet Mirroring: Full packet capture
- Network Intelligence Center: Topology, connectivity tests
- Performance Dashboard: Network latency and packet loss
- Use connectivity tests to debug routing issues

- Managed SSL certificates: Auto-provisioned and renewed by Google
- Self-managed: Upload your own certificates
- SSL policies: Control min TLS version and cipher suites
- Certificate Manager: Large-scale certificate management
- TLS 1.2+ recommended, TLS 1.0/1.1 deprecated

questions.question: [Hybrid Connectivity Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You need to configure dynamic routing between your on-premises network and a Google Cloud VPC using Cloud VPN. Which service is required to establish BGP sessions?
questions.explanation: Cloud Router uses Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) to dynamically exchange routes between your VPC and on-premises networks. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-network-engineer-q1)

questions.question: [VPC Organization Architecture Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Your enterprise wants to centralize network management (subnets, firewalls, routing) in a single host project while allowing developers to launch VMs in separate service projects. What should you configure?
questions.explanation: Shared VPC allows an organization to connect resources from multiple projects to a common Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) network. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-network-engineer-q2)

questions.question: [IP Address Management Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You have VM instances in a private subnet (no public IPs) that need to download updates from the public internet. Which service should you configure to enable this internet egress?
questions.explanation: Cloud NAT (Network Address Translation) allows VM instances without external IP addresses to access the internet. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-network-engineer-q3)

questions.question: [Load Balancing Traffic Routing Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You are hosting a video streaming service. You want to cache content at edge locations to minimize latency for global clients. Which load balancing feature should you enable?
questions.explanation: Cloud CDN (Content Delivery Network) works with external HTTP(S) load balancers to cache media content close to users. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-network-engineer-q4)

questions.question: [Domain Name Resolution Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to forward DNS queries from your on-premises active directory domain to your private Google Cloud DNS zones. What should you configure?
options: A hosts file on the instances
questions.explanation: An Inbound DNS Query Policy allows you to configure entry-point IP addresses in your VPC that resolve queries via Cloud DNS. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-network-engineer-q5)

certifications.name: Google Professional Cloud DevOps Engineer
certifications.description: Evaluates proficiency in using Google Cloud services to build and operationalize software delivery pipelines and manage service reliability.

cheatsheet.summary: DevOps and SRE blueprint validating deployment automations, CI/CD canary strategies, SLI/SLO metric configurations, error budget management, logging routers, and IaC provisioning.

coreConcepts.name: SLIs, SLOs, and SLAs
coreConcepts.desc: SLI (Service Level Indicator, e.g. latency). SLO (Service Level Objective, e.g. latency < 200ms for 99.9% of calls). SLA (Service Level Agreement, legal contract with penalties).

coreConcepts.name: Error Budget Management
coreConcepts.desc: The acceptable level of failure (100% - SLO). Used to balance feature deployment speed against system stability. Budget depletion pauses deployments.

coreConcepts.name: Canary & Blue-Green Releases
coreConcepts.desc: Canary rolls out updates to a small subset of servers or users first. Blue-Green switches traffic instantly from green (old) to blue (new) target groups.

coreConcepts.name: Infrastructure as Code (IaC)
coreConcepts.desc: Using Terraform to define, version, and provision GCP infrastructure. Prevents configuration drift and automates environment setups.

coreConcepts.name: Logging Routers & Sinks
coreConcepts.desc: Routes logs from projects, folders, or organizations to BigQuery for SQL analysis, Pub/Sub for real-time alerting, or GCS for long-term audit storage.

commands.desc: Initializes and provisions declared resources using local state configurations.
Initiates a release deployment process using Cloud Deploy delivery configurations.
Creates a Log Sink to export error logs to BigQuery.

architecturalPatterns.solution: Configure Cloud Deploy targeting GKE. Deploy code to 10% target. Monitor SLIs/SLOs in Cloud Monitoring. Auto-promote to 100% if SLOs remain intact.
Manage resources in Git. A merge to main triggers Cloud Build to run Terraform plan, output audits to Cloud Logging, and execute terraform apply.

flashcards.back:
- Define Service Level Indicators (SLIs) for key services
- Set Service Level Objectives (SLOs) to measure target availability
- Manage Error Budgets (100% - SLO) to balance speed and safety
- Lead blameless post-mortems for production outages

- Automate packaging using Cloud Build docker commands
- Provision resources programmatically via Terraform IaC state files
- Deploy software with Cloud Deploy canary rollouts
- Store packages securely in Artifact Registry

- Create dashboards and Alerting policies in Cloud Monitoring
- Export log files via Log Sinks to BigQuery
- Profile code latency with Cloud Profiler
- Route logs to Cloud Storage buckets for compliance archiving

study_flashcards.back:
- Serverless CI/CD platform
- Build, test, and deploy via YAML/JSON config (cloudbuild.yaml)
- Supports Docker, Maven, Gradle, npm, Go, and more
- Triggers: GitHub, Cloud Source Repos, Pub/Sub, webhooks
- 120 free build-minutes/day, parallel builds supported

- Managed continuous delivery service for GKE and Cloud Run
- Delivery pipelines with promotion through environments
- Supports canary, blue-green, and rolling deployments
- Approval gates between stages (dev → staging → prod)
- Integrates with Cloud Build for CI

- Error budget = 1 - SLO (e.g., 99.9% SLO = 0.1% error budget)
- Represents allowed unreliability per period
- If budget is spent: freeze features, focus on reliability
- If budget remains: ship new features faster
- Aligns development velocity with reliability goals

- Deployment Frequency: How often code is deployed
- Lead Time for Changes: Code commit to production
- Change Failure Rate: Percentage of deployments causing failures
- Time to Restore Service: Recovery time from incidents
- Elite teams: Multiple deploys/day, < 1 hour lead time

- Latency: Time to serve a request
- Traffic: Amount of demand on the system
- Errors: Rate of failed requests
- Saturation: How full the system is (CPU, memory, disk)
- Focus dashboards and alerts on these four signals

- Cloud Trace: Distributed tracing for request latency analysis
- Shows end-to-end request flow across microservices
- Cloud Profiler: Continuous CPU/memory profiling in production
- Identifies performance bottlenecks in code
- Trace for latency issues; Profiler for code-level optimization

study_flashcards.front: Blue-Green vs Canary vs Rolling deployments?
study_flashcards.back:
- Blue-Green: Two identical environments, switch traffic instantly
- Canary: Route small % of traffic to new version, gradually increase
- Rolling: Replace instances incrementally, no extra resources
- Blue-Green: Fastest rollback, most resources needed
- Canary: Safest for risk detection, slower rollout

- Define incident severity levels (P1-P4)
- Incident Commander coordinates response
- Use runbooks for common failure scenarios
- Blameless post-mortems after every significant incident
- Document: Timeline, root cause, impact, action items
- Cloud Error Reporting auto-groups application errors

study_flashcards.front: Infrastructure as Code tools on GCP?
study_flashcards.back:
- Terraform: Multi-cloud, HCL language, state management
- Cloud Deployment Manager: GCP-native, YAML templates
- Pulumi: Programming language-based IaC
- Config Connector: Manage GCP resources via Kubernetes
- Best practice: Version control all infrastructure configs

- Ensures only trusted containers are deployed to GKE
- Requires attestations (digital signatures) on container images
- Policy enforcement at deploy time
- Integrates with Artifact Registry vulnerability scanning
- Break-glass mode for emergency deployments

- Cloud Monitoring: Metrics, dashboards, alerting
- Cloud Logging: Centralized log management
- Cloud Trace: Distributed request tracing
- Cloud Profiler: Code-level performance profiling
- Error Reporting: Automatic error grouping and notification
- Cloud Debugger (deprecated): Replaced by snapshot debugging

- Deliberately inject failures to test system resilience
- Test: Network partitions, VM crashes, zone outages
- Verify auto-healing, failover, and alerting work correctly
- Start with game days (planned chaos experiments)
- Tools: Chaos Monkey, Litmus, Gremlin
- Run in pre-production first, then production with safeguards

- Create custom metrics from log entries via filters
- Counter metrics: Count log entries matching a filter
- Distribution metrics: Track numeric values from logs
- Use log-based metrics to trigger Cloud Monitoring alerts
- Example: Alert on error count exceeding threshold

- Unit tests: Individual function/method validation
- Integration tests: Service interaction verification
- Load tests: Capacity and performance under stress
- Canary analysis: Compare canary metrics to baseline
- Disaster recovery drills: Validate failover procedures
- Shift-left: Test early in development pipeline

- Artifact Registry: Current, recommended service
- Supports Docker, Maven, npm, Python, Go, Apt, Yum
- Regional and multi-regional repositories
- Vulnerability scanning with Artifact Analysis
- Container Registry: Legacy, Docker-only, being deprecated
- Always use Artifact Registry for new projects

questions.question: [Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You are configuring alerts for a web service. You want to measure the percentage of successful HTTP requests over a 30-day window to ensure reliability. What is this metric called in SRE?
questions.explanation: An SLI is a quantifiable metric of service performance (like successful request ratio). The target (like 99.9%) is the SLO. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-devops-engineer-q1)

questions.question: [Monitoring and Alerting Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to notify your on-call team via PagerDuty whenever your application's error rate exceeds 5% for consecutive minutes. Where should you configure this?
questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring alerting policies allow you to define metric thresholds and direct notifications to PagerDuty, email, or Slack. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-devops-engineer-q2)

questions.question: [Deployment Strategies Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to deploy a new version of an application to a subset of users first to verify stability before rolling it out to all users. Which deployment strategy does this represent?
questions.explanation: A canary deployment routes a small percentage of production traffic to the new version to check for errors before full deployment. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-devops-engineer-q3)

questions.question: [Infrastructure as Code Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to provision, manage, and version-control your Google Cloud infrastructure in a declarative template format. What is the industry-standard tool supported by Google Cloud providers?
questions.explanation: Terraform is an open-source, declarative Infrastructure-as-Code tool widely used to manage Google Cloud resources. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-cloud-devops-engineer-q4)

questions.question: [Log Analysis Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to perform real-time SQL analysis on millions of application logs stored in Cloud Logging. What should you configure?
options: Upgrade the log bucket to use Log Analytics and query it via SQL.
Export logs to a Cloud Storage bucket and run Python scripts.
Filter logs in the Log Explorer UI manually.
Configure a Log Sink to Cloud SQL.

certifications.name: Google Professional Machine Learning Engineer
certifications.description: Tests your ability to design, build, and productionize ML models on GCP using Vertex AI.

cheatsheet.summary: ML engineering blueprint focusing on model design, distributed training, pipeline integrations (Vertex AI), prediction endpoints, MLOps, and model drift tracking.

coreConcepts.name: Vertex AI Pipelines & SDK
coreConcepts.desc: Coordinates ML workflows using Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP) or TensorFlow Extended (TFX) to orchestrate data loading, training, and evaluations.

coreConcepts.name: Model Training Architectures
coreConcepts.desc: Custom containers run custom model code. Distributed training scales across multiple compute instances using GPUs or Tensor Processing Units (TPUs).

coreConcepts.name: Model Deployments & Serving
coreConcepts.desc: Deploys model files to Vertex AI Endpoints for low-latency online predictions, or triggers Batch Prediction jobs for processing high-volume datasets.

coreConcepts.name: MLOps, Skew, and Drift Monitoring
coreConcepts.desc: Skew is the difference between training and serving data distributions. Drift is the change in serving data distributions over time. Vertex AI monitors both.

coreConcepts.name: Feature Store Management
coreConcepts.desc: Vertex AI Feature Store provides a centralized repository to store, share, and serve machine learning features, ensuring feature consistency between training and online serving.

commands.desc: Submits a custom model training container job to Vertex AI.
Sends live feature inputs to a deployed Vertex AI model endpoint for online predictions.

architecturalPatterns.solution: Run a daily pipeline in Vertex AI Pipelines that extracts transactional data from BigQuery, processes features, trains an XGBoost model, and registers it in the Model Registry.
Deploy model to an Endpoint. Configure Vertex AI Model Monitoring to sample live inference traffic and send email alerts if feature drift thresholds are breached.

flashcards.back:
- Select GCP ML services (AutoML vs Custom Vertex AI jobs)
- Choose compute types (CPUs, GPUs, TPUs) for training
- Plan data ingestion pipelines from BigQuery
- Structure feature sharing with Vertex AI Feature Store

- Package code in custom containers for Vertex AI custom training
- Scale training using distributed clusters
- Search hyperparameters using Vertex AI Vizier jobs
- Manage training experiments in Vertex AI Pipelines (Kubeflow)

- Deploy model artifacts to Vertex AI endpoints for online predictions
- Run batch prediction jobs for high-volume logs
- Monitor endpoints for training-serving data skew
- Detect feature distribution drift over time

study_flashcards.back:
- Data prep: Vertex AI Datasets, Feature Store
- Training: AutoML or custom training with containers
- Evaluation: Model evaluation metrics dashboard
- Deployment: Vertex AI Endpoints (online) or batch prediction
- Monitoring: Model Monitoring for drift detection
- Pipelines: Vertex AI Pipelines for MLOps automation

study_flashcards.front: AutoML vs Custom Training on Vertex AI?
study_flashcards.back:
- AutoML: No-code, Google searches best architecture
- Custom: Full control, bring your own TensorFlow/PyTorch/XGBoost
- AutoML: Best for tabular, image, text, video when domain knowledge is limited
- Custom: Best when you need specific architectures or optimization
- AutoML supports edge deployment for mobile/IoT

- Centralized repository for ML features
- Serves features for training and online prediction consistently
- Prevents training-serving skew
- Supports point-in-time lookups for historical features
- Feature monitoring for drift detection
- Integrates with BigQuery for offline feature computation

- Oversampling: Duplicate minority class examples (SMOTE)
- Undersampling: Remove majority class examples
- Class weights: Penalize misclassification of minority class more
- Data augmentation: Create synthetic minority examples
- Evaluation: Use precision, recall, F1, AUC-ROC instead of accuracy
- Stratified sampling for train/test splits

- Data parallelism: Split data across workers, each has full model
- Model parallelism: Split model layers across workers
- Mirrored strategy: Synchronous training on multiple GPUs
- Parameter server: Async training with centralized parameters
- Vertex AI handles distributed training infrastructure
- Use when dataset or model is too large for single GPU

- Accuracy: Overall correct predictions (misleading for imbalanced data)
- Precision: True positives / (True positives + False positives)
- Recall: True positives / (True positives + False negatives)
- F1 Score: Harmonic mean of precision and recall
- AUC-ROC: Model's ability to distinguish classes
- RMSE/MAE: Regression metrics for continuous predictions

- Managed orchestration for ML workflows (based on KFP/TFX)
- Define pipeline as a DAG of components
- Components: Reusable, containerized tasks
- Automatic lineage tracking and metadata storage
- Scheduling: Run pipelines on cron or trigger-based
- Compare experiments and model versions

- Online: Real-time, low-latency predictions via REST/gRPC endpoint
- Batch: Process large datasets asynchronously
- Online: Use for user-facing applications
- Batch: Use for scoring large datasets, reports, recommendations
- Vertex AI Endpoints auto-scale based on traffic
- Batch predictions output to BigQuery or Cloud Storage

- Training-serving skew: Feature distribution differs between training and serving
- Prediction drift: Model output distribution changes over time
- Feature drift: Input feature distributions shift
- Vertex AI Model Monitoring: Automatic drift detection
- Set alerting thresholds for feature and prediction drift
- Retrain when drift exceeds acceptable bounds

- ExampleGen: Data ingestion and splitting
- StatisticsGen: Generate dataset statistics
- SchemaGen: Infer data schema
- ExampleValidator: Detect anomalies in data
- Transform: Feature engineering at scale
- Trainer: Model training
- Evaluator: Model evaluation and validation
- Pusher: Deploy validated models

- Vertex AI Vizier: Managed hyperparameter optimization
- Search algorithms: Bayesian, grid search, random search
- Early stopping: Terminate underperforming trials
- Objective metric: What to optimize (accuracy, loss, etc.)
- Parallel trials: Run multiple configurations simultaneously
- Transfer learning to reduce search space

- Feature attributions: Which features influenced predictions most
- Methods: Sampled Shapley, Integrated Gradients, XRAI
- Model cards: Document model performance and limitations
- Fairness indicators: Evaluate model across demographic groups
- What-If Tool: Interactive model exploration
- Required for regulatory compliance in many industries

- Pre-trained: Natural Language API (sentiment, entity, syntax)
- AutoML: Custom text classification and entity extraction
- Custom: Train BERT/T5 on Vertex AI custom training
- Embeddings: Text embeddings for semantic search
- LLMs: Gemini/PaLM via Vertex AI for generation tasks
- Document AI: Extract structured data from documents

- Normalization: Scale features to same range (StandardScaler, MinMaxScaler)
- Encoding: One-hot for categorical, embeddings for high-cardinality
- Bucketization: Convert continuous to discrete ranges
- Feature crosses: Combine features for non-linear relationships
- Handle missing values: Imputation, indicator variables
- Time features: Extract day-of-week, hour, season from timestamps

study_flashcards.front: GPU and TPU selection for ML training?
study_flashcards.back:
- GPU: NVIDIA T4 (inference), V100 (training), A100 (large models)
- TPU: Google's custom ASICs, optimized for TensorFlow/JAX
- TPU v4: 275 TFLOPS, best for large-scale training
- GPU: More flexible, supports PyTorch/TF/JAX
- TPU: Higher throughput for supported frameworks
- Preemptible/Spot GPUs: Up to 70% cheaper for training

questions.question: [Vertex AI Pipelines Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to orchestrate your machine learning workflow (data preprocessing, training, evaluation, deployment) in a serverless, reproducible pipeline. Which service should you choose?
questions.explanation: Vertex AI Pipelines is a serverless orchestration service that runs ML pipelines using Kubeflow Pipelines or TFX. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-machine-learning-engineer-q1)

questions.question: [Model Training Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You have a large tabular dataset and want to quickly find the best ML model architecture without writing custom TensorFlow or PyTorch training code. Which Vertex AI feature should you use?
questions.explanation: Vertex AI AutoML trains tabular, image, text, or video models automatically without requiring manual architecture design. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-machine-learning-engineer-q2)

questions.question: [Feature Management Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to store, share, and serve machine learning features across multiple training and online serving systems to avoid training-serving skew. Which Vertex AI service is designed for this?
questions.explanation: Vertex AI Feature Store provides a centralized repository for organizing, storing, and serving ML features. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-machine-learning-engineer-q3)

questions.question: [Model Monitoring Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Your deployed ML model's prediction accuracy has degraded over time because the distribution of incoming inference data has changed compared to the training data. What is this phenomenon called?
questions.explanation: Data Drift occurs when input data properties change over time, resulting in degraded model performance (monitored by Vertex AI Model Monitoring). (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-machine-learning-engineer-q4)

questions.question: [Data Ingestion Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You are training a deep learning model on petabytes of image data. You want to stream data from Cloud Storage into your training jobs efficiently to prevent GPU starvation. Which data format is recommended?
questions.explanation: The TFRecord format is a simple record-oriented binary format optimized for high-throughput streaming pipelines in TensorFlow. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-machine-learning-engineer-q5)

questions.question: [Vertex AI Pipelines Scenario 2] In your company's Project B environment: You want to orchestrate your machine learning workflow (data preprocessing, training, evaluation, deployment) in a serverless, reproducible pipeline. Which service should you choose?
questions.explanation: Vertex AI Pipelines is a serverless orchestration service that runs ML pipelines using Kubeflow Pipelines or TFX. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-machine-learning-engineer-q6)

questions.question: [Model Training Scenario 2] In your company's Project B environment: You have a large tabular dataset and want to quickly find the best ML model architecture without writing custom TensorFlow or PyTorch training code. Which Vertex AI feature should you use?
questions.explanation: Vertex AI AutoML trains tabular, image, text, or video models automatically without requiring manual architecture design. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-machine-learning-engineer-q7)

questions.question: [Feature Management Scenario 2] In your company's Project B environment: You want to store, share, and serve machine learning features across multiple training and online serving systems to avoid training-serving skew. Which Vertex AI service is designed for this?
questions.explanation: Vertex AI Feature Store provides a centralized repository for organizing, storing, and serving ML features. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: google-professional-machine-learning-engineer-q8)

certifications.name: dbt Certified Developer
certifications.description: Validates expertise in analytics engineering workflows, dbt compilation, modeling, testing, documentation, and deployment.

cheatsheet.summary: Advanced software engineering practices with dbt. Covers dynamic Jinja scripting, packages manager, CI/CD slim runs, custom schema architectures, and metadata queries.

coreConcepts.name: Advanced Jinja & Adapter Macros
coreConcepts.desc: Using Jinja control flow and loop structures. Utilizing adapter methods like 'adapter.dispatch()' to run distinct SQL based on database engine.

coreConcepts.name: dbt Package Management
coreConcepts.desc: Imports open-source packages (e.g., dbt_utils) by declaring dependencies in 'packages.yml' and running install commands to import macros.

coreConcepts.name: Slim CI/CD Deployments
coreConcepts.desc: dbt Cloud CI runs only compile and test modified models and their downstream dependencies using 'state:modified+' options, saving warehouse compute costs.

coreConcepts.name: Custom Schema Configurations
coreConcepts.desc: Override default schema placements. Configure 'generate_schema_name' macro to route staging models to dev schemas and marts to production schemas.

coreConcepts.name: Artifacts & Metadata APIs
coreConcepts.desc: dbt runs compile files like 'manifest.json' and 'run_results.json'. These artifacts can be parsed to audit execution times and model dependencies.

commands.desc: Downloads and installs external dbt packages configured in packages.yml.
Validates database connection credentials, profiles file paths, and local project configuration variables.
Runs only modified models and downstream dependencies in CI, deferring unchanged tables to production schemas.

architecturalPatterns.solution: Create a macro that uses 'adapter.dispatch()'. Define different SQL syntaxes for BigQuery and Snowflake to handle date calculations dynamically.
Integrate GitHub Actions with dbt Cloud. A PR run compiles only changed code, deferring to the production manifest to run slim CI checks.

flashcards.back:
- Write dynamic SQL query files using Jinja loops and conditional blocks
- Author reusable macros in the macros/ folder
- Dispatch query structures dynamically based on database adapters
- Leverage env variables inside dbt project config files

- Declare external package dependencies inside packages.yml
- Download packages (e.g. dbt_utils) using 'dbt deps'
- Override packages default macros with custom definitions
- Leverage package-defined data tests and schemas

flashcards.front: Slim CI deployments in dbt Cloud
flashcards.back:
- Run only modified models using '--select state:modified+'
- Defer unchanged models to production manifest.json artifact targets
- Configure automated PR checks in dbt Cloud jobs
- Run data tests in CI against scratch database schemas

- Configure model clustering and sorting in dbt config blocks
- Configure BigQuery partition strategies in models yml
- Optimize table structures with dbt incremental materializations
- Monitor model runtimes using catalog manifest artifacts

study_flashcards.front: What is dbt and what does it do?
study_flashcards.back:
- dbt (data build tool): SQL-based transformation framework
- Transforms raw data in the warehouse (ELT, not ETL)
- Models are SELECT statements that dbt materializes
- Supports: Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Databricks, PostgreSQL
- Enables version control, testing, and documentation for analytics

- table: DROP + CREATE TABLE AS (full refresh)
- view: CREATE VIEW AS (computed on query)
- incremental: INSERT/MERGE only new or changed rows
- ephemeral: CTE inlined into downstream models (not materialized)
- Use table for small static data, incremental for large datasets
- Use view for lightweight transformations

- ref('model_name'): Reference another dbt model
- source('source_name', 'table_name'): Reference raw source tables
- ref() creates a DAG dependency between models
- source() integrates with source freshness tests
- Always use ref() to reference other models, never hardcode table names
- Both handle schema/database resolution automatically

study_flashcards.front: dbt testing: Schema vs Data tests?
study_flashcards.back:
- Schema tests: Defined in YAML (unique, not_null, accepted_values, relationships)
- Data tests: Custom SQL queries in tests/ directory
- Generic tests: Reusable test macros with parameters
- Tests run with 'dbt test' command
- Failed tests return rows that violate the assertion
- Severity: warn or error (configurable per test)

- Defined in YAML with loaded_at_field and freshness thresholds
- warn_after and error_after with count and period
- Run with 'dbt source freshness' command
- Checks if source data is stale based on loaded_at column
- Integrates with CI/CD to alert on data pipeline issues
- Results stored in target/sources.json

- Jinja: Templating language for dynamic SQL
- {{ }} for expressions, {% %} for statements, {# #} for comments
- Macros: Reusable Jinja functions defined in macros/ directory
- Built-in macros: date_spine, generate_schema_name, etc.
- dbt_utils package: Common utility macros
- Use macros to DRY up repetitive SQL patterns

- Describe models, columns, and tests in YAML files
- doc blocks: Markdown documentation in .md files
- 'dbt docs generate' creates documentation site
- 'dbt docs serve' hosts documentation locally
- Auto-generated lineage graph (DAG visualization)
- Column-level descriptions show in data catalog tools

- External reusable dbt code (models, macros, tests)
- Defined in packages.yml at project root
- Install with 'dbt deps' command
- Popular: dbt_utils, dbt_expectations, codegen
- Hub packages from hub.getdbt.com
- Git packages from private or public repositories

- append: INSERT new rows only (fastest, no updates)
- merge: MERGE/upsert based on unique_key (handles updates)
- delete+insert: Delete matching rows, then insert
- insert_overwrite: Replace entire partitions
- is_incremental() macro: Differentiate full vs incremental runs
- --full-refresh flag forces complete rebuild

- staging/: 1:1 with source tables, light transformations
- intermediate/: Business logic joins and aggregations
- marts/: Final business entities for consumers
- sources.yml: Source definitions with freshness and tests
- schema.yml: Model and column descriptions and tests
- Naming: stg_source__table, int_subject__verb, fct/dim_entity

- Track historical changes in source data (Slowly Changing Dimensions)
- strategy: timestamp or check
- timestamp: Uses updated_at column to detect changes
- check: Compares column values for changes
- Adds dbt_valid_from, dbt_valid_to, dbt_scd_id columns
- Run with 'dbt snapshot', defined in snapshots/ directory

- CSV files stored in the seeds/ directory of your project
- Load into warehouse with 'dbt seed' command
- Use for small, static reference data (country codes, mappings)
- NOT for large datasets (use sources instead)
- Can override column types in dbt_project.yml
- Version-controlled with your dbt project

- dbt run: Execute all models
- dbt test: Run all tests
- dbt build: Run + test in DAG order
- dbt compile: Generate SQL without executing
- dbt deps: Install packages from packages.yml
- dbt run --select model_name: Run specific model
- dbt run --select tag:daily: Run models with tag

- pre-hook: SQL executed before a model runs
- post-hook: SQL executed after a model runs
- on-run-start: SQL at beginning of dbt run
- on-run-end: SQL at end of dbt run
- Use for: GRANT permissions, ANALYZE tables, custom logging
- Defined in dbt_project.yml or model config blocks

- Exposures: Document downstream consumers (dashboards, ML models)
- Define in YAML with type, owner, depends_on
- Shows in DAG which models feed which business tools
- Metrics: Define business metrics in YAML (deprecated in favor of Semantic Layer)
- dbt Semantic Layer: Centralized metric definitions for BI tools
- Ensures consistent metric calculations across the organization

questions.question: [dbt Core Concepts Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: In a dbt project, what programming languages are primarily used to define models?
questions.explanation: dbt combines SQL and Jinja (a templating language) to write modular database transformations. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-developer-q1)

questions.question: [dbt Compilation Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Where does dbt execute the data transformation models when you run 'dbt run'?
options: On the client computer running the dbt CLI
Inside a dedicated dbt cloud server
Directly inside the target data warehouse (e.g., Snowflake, BigQuery)
questions.explanation: dbt does not process data itself. It compiles your code into SQL and sends it to your target database/data warehouse for execution. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-developer-q2)

questions.question: [dbt Materializations Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Which dbt materialization configuration compiles the model SQL into a physical table that is completely dropped and rebuilt on every execution?
questions.explanation: The 'table' materialization rebuilds the model as a physical table by dropping the old table and running a CREATE TABLE AS statement. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-developer-q3)

questions.question: [dbt Tests Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Which command compiles and executes data tests defined in your dbt project schema files?
questions.explanation: The `dbt test` command runs assertions against your models to validate data quality. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-developer-q4)

questions.question: [dbt Lineage Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: What is the primary function of the ref() function in dbt models?
options: It references external source raw tables.
It establishes lineage dependencies between models, enabling dbt to build models in the correct order.
It executes a sub-query directly from python pandas.
questions.explanation: The `ref()` function allows you to reference other models and tells dbt how to construct the DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) to compile dependencies. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-developer-q5)

questions.question: [dbt Core Concepts Scenario 2] In your company's Project B environment: In a dbt project, what programming languages are primarily used to define models?
questions.explanation: dbt combines SQL and Jinja (a templating language) to write modular database transformations. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-developer-q6)

questions.question: [dbt Compilation Scenario 2] In your company's Project B environment: Where does dbt execute the data transformation models when you run 'dbt run'?
options: On the client computer running the dbt CLI
Inside a dedicated dbt cloud server
Directly inside the target data warehouse (e.g., Snowflake, BigQuery)
questions.explanation: dbt does not process data itself. It compiles your code into SQL and sends it to your target database/data warehouse for execution. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-developer-q7)

certifications.name: dbt Certified Analytics Engineer
certifications.description: Tests advanced competency in structuring warehouses, performance optimization, package management, custom macros, and advanced lineage.

cheatsheet.summary: Analytics engineering study guide focusing on SQL modeling, Jinja macro structures, model materializations, dependencies (ref/source), test configurations, and slowly changing dimensions (Snapshots).

coreConcepts.name: dbt Project Structure
coreConcepts.desc: Configured in 'dbt_project.yml'. Main directories: models/ (SQL files), seeds/ (small static CSVs), macros/ (reusable Jinja functions), analyses/ (one-off SQL).

coreConcepts.name: dbt Materialization Types
coreConcepts.desc: View (default, virtual table), Table (re-built on each run), Incremental (appends new records based on filters), Ephemeral (nested as inline CTEs).

coreConcepts.name: Refs & Sources (DAG Building)
coreConcepts.desc: The ref() function links models, building the Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) for dependencies. The source() function references raw source tables in the data warehouse.

coreConcepts.name: Testing (Schema vs Data)
coreConcepts.desc: Schema tests validate constraints (unique, not_null, accepted_values, relationships) inside YAML files. Data tests are custom SQL files that return failing rows.

coreConcepts.name: dbt Seeds vs Snapshots
coreConcepts.desc: Seeds are static lookup tables compiled from CSV files. Snapshots capture change history over time, implementing SCD Type 2 columns (valid_from, valid_to).

commands.desc: Compiles dbt models into raw SQL and executes them, materializing tables/views in the target data warehouse.
Runs all configured tests against materialized database tables, outputting failures in the console.
Translates dbt Jinja SQL files into raw SQL code, locating outputs in the target/ folder without executing them.

commands.cmd: dbt docs generate && dbt docs serve
commands.desc: Builds documentation JSON files from project yml descriptions and launches a local documentation server.
Runs the specified model and all of its downstream dependencies.

architecturalPatterns.solution: Ingest raw transaction logs. Build staging models (data type casting), intermediate models (aggregating dimensions), and final mart tables for BI tools.
Configure a model with incremental materialization. Use Jinja block 'is_incremental()' to load only rows newer than the max active date in the target table.

flashcards.back:
- Define data warehouse layers: staging, intermediate, and marts
- Setup dbt sources using source() to isolate raw data references
- Build DAG dependencies using ref() to direct execution sequences
- Configure model materializations: views, tables, and CTEs

- Configure incremental materializations to append recent rows only
- Filter incremental runs using Jinja 'is_incremental()' block
- Nest models as ephemeral CTEs to avoid creating physical views
- Troubleshoot full-refresh runs when database schemas update

- Configure schema tests (unique, not_null, accepted_values, relationships)
- Write custom data tests in tests/ returning failed rows
- Build project documentation descriptions in yml schema files
- Generate docs locally using 'dbt docs generate'

- Implement dbt snapshots in the snapshots/ folder
- Configure snapshots to track SCD Type 2 changes over time
- Define check strategies (timestamp vs check_cols)
- Query snapshot tables for historical time-range analysis

study_flashcards.front: What is dbt and what does it do?
study_flashcards.back:
- dbt (data build tool): SQL-based transformation framework
- Transforms raw data in the warehouse (ELT, not ETL)
- Models are SELECT statements that dbt materializes
- Supports: Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Databricks, PostgreSQL
- Enables version control, testing, and documentation for analytics

- table: DROP + CREATE TABLE AS (full refresh)
- view: CREATE VIEW AS (computed on query)
- incremental: INSERT/MERGE only new or changed rows
- ephemeral: CTE inlined into downstream models (not materialized)
- Use table for small static data, incremental for large datasets
- Use view for lightweight transformations

- ref('model_name'): Reference another dbt model
- source('source_name', 'table_name'): Reference raw source tables
- ref() creates a DAG dependency between models
- source() integrates with source freshness tests
- Always use ref() to reference other models, never hardcode table names
- Both handle schema/database resolution automatically

study_flashcards.front: dbt testing: Schema vs Data tests?
study_flashcards.back:
- Schema tests: Defined in YAML (unique, not_null, accepted_values, relationships)
- Data tests: Custom SQL queries in tests/ directory
- Generic tests: Reusable test macros with parameters
- Tests run with 'dbt test' command
- Failed tests return rows that violate the assertion
- Severity: warn or error (configurable per test)

- Defined in YAML with loaded_at_field and freshness thresholds
- warn_after and error_after with count and period
- Run with 'dbt source freshness' command
- Checks if source data is stale based on loaded_at column
- Integrates with CI/CD to alert on data pipeline issues
- Results stored in target/sources.json

- Jinja: Templating language for dynamic SQL
- {{ }} for expressions, {% %} for statements, {# #} for comments
- Macros: Reusable Jinja functions defined in macros/ directory
- Built-in macros: date_spine, generate_schema_name, etc.
- dbt_utils package: Common utility macros
- Use macros to DRY up repetitive SQL patterns

- Describe models, columns, and tests in YAML files
- doc blocks: Markdown documentation in .md files
- 'dbt docs generate' creates documentation site
- 'dbt docs serve' hosts documentation locally
- Auto-generated lineage graph (DAG visualization)
- Column-level descriptions show in data catalog tools

- External reusable dbt code (models, macros, tests)
- Defined in packages.yml at project root
- Install with 'dbt deps' command
- Popular: dbt_utils, dbt_expectations, codegen
- Hub packages from hub.getdbt.com
- Git packages from private or public repositories

- append: INSERT new rows only (fastest, no updates)
- merge: MERGE/upsert based on unique_key (handles updates)
- delete+insert: Delete matching rows, then insert
- insert_overwrite: Replace entire partitions
- is_incremental() macro: Differentiate full vs incremental runs
- --full-refresh flag forces complete rebuild

- staging/: 1:1 with source tables, light transformations
- intermediate/: Business logic joins and aggregations
- marts/: Final business entities for consumers
- sources.yml: Source definitions with freshness and tests
- schema.yml: Model and column descriptions and tests
- Naming: stg_source__table, int_subject__verb, fct/dim_entity

- Track historical changes in source data (Slowly Changing Dimensions)
- strategy: timestamp or check
- timestamp: Uses updated_at column to detect changes
- check: Compares column values for changes
- Adds dbt_valid_from, dbt_valid_to, dbt_scd_id columns
- Run with 'dbt snapshot', defined in snapshots/ directory

- CSV files stored in the seeds/ directory of your project
- Load into warehouse with 'dbt seed' command
- Use for small, static reference data (country codes, mappings)
- NOT for large datasets (use sources instead)
- Can override column types in dbt_project.yml
- Version-controlled with your dbt project

- dbt run: Execute all models
- dbt test: Run all tests
- dbt build: Run + test in DAG order
- dbt compile: Generate SQL without executing
- dbt deps: Install packages from packages.yml
- dbt run --select model_name: Run specific model
- dbt run --select tag:daily: Run models with tag

- pre-hook: SQL executed before a model runs
- post-hook: SQL executed after a model runs
- on-run-start: SQL at beginning of dbt run
- on-run-end: SQL at end of dbt run
- Use for: GRANT permissions, ANALYZE tables, custom logging
- Defined in dbt_project.yml or model config blocks

- Exposures: Document downstream consumers (dashboards, ML models)
- Define in YAML with type, owner, depends_on
- Shows in DAG which models feed which business tools
- Metrics: Define business metrics in YAML (deprecated in favor of Semantic Layer)
- dbt Semantic Layer: Centralized metric definitions for BI tools
- Ensures consistent metric calculations across the organization

questions.question: [Package Management Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to utilize pre-built dbt tests and utility macros (like surrogate_key) created by the dbt community. In which configuration file must you declare these package dependencies?
questions.explanation: dbt packages are declared in a `packages.yml` file in the root of the project, and installed using the `dbt deps` command. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-analytics-engineer-q1)

questions.question: [Custom Macros Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to write reusable Jinja logic to pivot columns or dynamically generate date ranges. Where should you define this code inside your dbt project?
options: In a .sql file in the macros/ directory
In a .yml file in the models/ directory
questions.explanation: Custom dbt macros are written in SQL files within the `macros/` folder using Jinja `{% macro ... %}` syntax. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-analytics-engineer-q2)

questions.question: [Incremental Models Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You are building a high-volume incremental model. You want to avoid duplicate records by overwriting existing rows based on a unique key, utilizing a merge statement. Which incremental strategy configuration should you define?
questions.explanation: The `merge` incremental strategy (supported on warehouses like Snowflake and BigQuery) matches rows using a unique key to update existing records and insert new ones. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-analytics-engineer-q3)

questions.question: [dbt Source Freshness Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: You want to monitor the freshness of your raw source data to ensure your data pipeline has loaded records within the last 6 hours. Which command should you schedule?
questions.explanation: The `dbt source freshness` command uses configurations in your source schema YAML files to query the maximum timestamp of your raw data. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-analytics-engineer-q4)

questions.question: [Custom Materializations Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: What is the correct way to define a custom materialization (e.g. creating index structures or custom tables) in dbt?
options: Use the {% materialization %} block inside a SQL file in your macros/ directory.
Edit the dbt core source code in python.
Configure it directly inside dbt_project.yml under model configs.
Custom materializations are not supported in dbt.
questions.explanation: dbt allows developers to write custom materializations by defining a `{% materialization ... %}` block in a macro SQL file. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-analytics-engineer-q5)

questions.question: [Package Management Scenario 2] In your company's Project B environment: You want to utilize pre-built dbt tests and utility macros (like surrogate_key) created by the dbt community. In which configuration file must you declare these package dependencies?
questions.explanation: dbt packages are declared in a `packages.yml` file in the root of the project, and installed using the `dbt deps` command. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-analytics-engineer-q6)

questions.question: [Custom Macros Scenario 2] In your company's Project B environment: You want to write reusable Jinja logic to pivot columns or dynamically generate date ranges. Where should you define this code inside your dbt project?
options: In a .sql file in the macros/ directory
In a .yml file in the models/ directory
questions.explanation: Custom dbt macros are written in SQL files within the `macros/` folder using Jinja `{% macro ... %}` syntax. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: dbt-certified-analytics-engineer-q7)

questions.question: [Incremental Models Scenario 2] In your company's Project B environment: You are building a high-volume incremental model. You want to avoid duplicate records by overwriting existing rows based on a unique key, utilizing a merge statement. Which incremental strategy configuration should you define?

certifications.name: AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner
certifications.description: Provides a high-level overview of AWS cloud concepts, security, technology, architecture, and billing models.

cheatsheet.summary: Foundational AWS credentials validating core global infrastructure, compute, storage, databases, IAM security models, billing features, and pricing structures.

coreConcepts.name: AWS Global Infrastructure
coreConcepts.desc: Regions (geographical clusters of AZs), Availability Zones (AZs, isolated datacenters), Edge Locations (caching destinations for CloudFront CDN).

coreConcepts.name: Compute Services Portfolio
coreConcepts.desc: Amazon EC2 (IaaS VMs), AWS Lambda (serverless event-driven functions), Amazon ECS/EKS (managed container platforms).

coreConcepts.name: Storage Services Portfolio
coreConcepts.desc: Amazon S3 (object storage), Amazon EBS (block storage volumes for EC2), Amazon EFS (managed shared network file systems).

coreConcepts.name: Databases Portfolio
coreConcepts.desc: Amazon RDS (managed relational DB), Amazon DynamoDB (serverless NoSQL document DB), Amazon Aurora (high-performance relational DB).

coreConcepts.name: AWS Shared Responsibility Model
coreConcepts.desc: AWS manages security 'of' the cloud (datacenter infrastructure). The customer manages security 'in' the cloud (data configurations, IAM users, firewall groups).

coreConcepts.name: AWS IAM Identity Management
coreConcepts.desc: AWS Identity and Access Management. Controls user logins, policies, and permissions. Enforces multi-factor authentication (MFA).

coreConcepts.name: Billing & Budgeting Tools
coreConcepts.desc: AWS Billing Dashboard, AWS Budgets (configures spend alert thresholds), AWS Cost Explorer (analyzes historical usage trends).

commands.desc: Prompts to configure AWS CLI access credentials, default region, and output formatting.
Creates a new Amazon S3 bucket globally.
Launches a basic EC2 instance in the configured subnet.

architecturalPatterns.solution: Deploy an Application Load Balancer to distribute traffic to EC2 instances across multiple Availability Zones, backed by an RDS Multi-AZ database.
Store static website files in an S3 bucket. Front the bucket with Amazon CloudFront CDN to cache content globally at edge locations.

flashcards.back:
- AWS Regions: geographical clusters of isolated Availability Zones
- Availability Zones: physically distinct datacenters within a region
- Edge Locations: caching endpoints for CloudFront CDN
- AWS Local Zones: run latency-sensitive apps close to users

- Compute: EC2 (VMs), AWS Lambda (serverless), ECS/EKS (containers)
- Storage: Amazon S3 (objects), EBS (disks), EFS (shared network files)
- Databases: RDS (managed SQL), DynamoDB (NoSQL document), Aurora (PaaS)

- AWS managers security OF the cloud (physical centers, network)
- Customer manages security IN the cloud (IAM users, firewalls, data)
- Enforce password policies and MFA in AWS IAM console
- Delegate access securely using IAM Roles

- AWS Cost Explorer: analyze historical costs and usage patterns
- AWS Budgets: configure custom budget spend alert notifications
- AWS Organizations: consolidate billing across multiple accounts
- Pricing options: Reserved Instances, Savings Plans, and Spot instances

study_flashcards.front: EC2 instance types and use cases?
study_flashcards.back:
- General Purpose (T3, M5): Balanced compute, memory, networking
- Compute Optimized (C5): High-performance processing, batch
- Memory Optimized (R5, X1): In-memory caching, databases
- Storage Optimized (I3, D2): High sequential read/write
- Accelerated Computing (P3, G4): GPU for ML/graphics
- T3 burstable: Earn credits when idle, burst when needed

- On-Demand: Pay by second, no commitment
- Reserved Instances: 1-3 year commit, up to 72% savings
- Spot Instances: Up to 90% off, can be reclaimed with 2-min warning
- Savings Plans: Flexible commitment (compute or EC2-specific)
- Dedicated Hosts: Physical servers for compliance
- Dedicated Instances: Isolated hardware, per-instance billing

- Standard: Frequently accessed, 99.99% availability
- Intelligent-Tiering: Auto-moves between tiers based on access
- Standard-IA: Infrequent access, lower cost, retrieval fee
- One Zone-IA: Single AZ, 20% less than Standard-IA
- Glacier Instant: Archive with millisecond retrieval
- Glacier Flexible: Minutes-hours retrieval
- Glacier Deep Archive: Cheapest, 12-48 hour retrieval

- gp3: General Purpose SSD, 3000 IOPS baseline, 125 MB/s
- gp2: General Purpose SSD, burstable to 3000 IOPS
- io2 Block Express: Provisioned IOPS, up to 256K IOPS
- st1: Throughput Optimized HDD, big data workloads
- sc1: Cold HDD, lowest cost for infrequent access
- Boot volumes: Only gp2, gp3, io1, io2 supported

- VPC: Regional virtual network
- Subnets: AZ-specific, public (with IGW route) or private
- Internet Gateway (IGW): Allows internet access for VPC
- NAT Gateway: Outbound internet for private subnets
- Route Tables: Control traffic routing
- Security Groups: Stateful, instance-level firewall
- NACLs: Stateless, subnet-level firewall

- Security Groups: Stateful (return traffic auto-allowed), instance-level
- NACLs: Stateless (must allow both inbound and outbound), subnet-level
- SG: Allow rules only (implicit deny all)
- NACL: Allow AND deny rules, processed in order by rule number
- Best practice: Use SGs for primary security, NACLs as extra layer

- RDS: Managed relational DB (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server)
- Aurora: AWS-optimized relational, 5x MySQL / 3x PostgreSQL performance
- DynamoDB: Serverless NoSQL, single-digit ms latency
- RDS: Multi-AZ for HA, read replicas for scaling
- Aurora: Auto-scaling storage up to 128 TB, multi-master
- DynamoDB: Auto-scaling, global tables for multi-region

- Multi-AZ deployments for RDS, ElastiCache
- Auto Scaling Groups across AZs
- Application Load Balancer distributes traffic
- Route 53 health checks and failover routing
- S3 cross-region replication for data
- Aurora Global Database for cross-region DR

- Enable MFA for root account and all users
- Use IAM roles instead of access keys
- Principle of least privilege
- Use AWS Organizations SCPs for guardrails
- Rotate credentials regularly
- Use IAM Access Analyzer to identify unintended access

- Max execution time: 15 minutes
- Memory: 128 MB to 10 GB
- Deployment package: 50 MB zipped, 250 MB unzipped
- Concurrency: 1000 per region (adjustable)
- Triggers: API Gateway, S3, SQS, DynamoDB Streams, EventBridge
- Pay per invocation and duration (1ms billing)

- Redis: Persistence, replication, pub/sub, complex data types
- Memcached: Multi-threaded, simple caching, no persistence
- Redis: Supports Multi-AZ with automatic failover
- Memcached: Supports auto-discovery of nodes
- Redis: Best for leaderboards, sessions, real-time analytics
- Memcached: Best for simple key-value caching at scale

- SQS: Queue-based, consumer pulls messages, decouples services
- SNS: Pub/Sub, push-based fan-out to subscribers
- EventBridge: Event bus with rules and filtering
- SQS: At-least-once delivery (Standard) or exactly-once (FIFO)
- SNS: Topics → Email, SMS, Lambda, SQS, HTTP endpoints
- EventBridge: Event-driven architectures with 100+ AWS service sources

- Global CDN with 400+ edge locations
- Origins: S3, ALB, EC2, custom HTTP servers
- Lambda@Edge: Run code at edge locations
- Field-level encryption for sensitive data
- Signed URLs and cookies for private content
- Origin Access Identity (OAI): Restrict S3 access to CloudFront only

- AWS Migration Hub: Central tracking for migrations
- Application Migration Service: Lift-and-shift servers
- Database Migration Service (DMS): Migrate databases
- Schema Conversion Tool (SCT): Convert schemas between engines
- Snowball/Snowcone: Physical data transfer devices
- DataSync: Online data transfer for files and objects

- Metrics: CPU, network, disk, custom application metrics
- Alarms: Trigger actions based on metric thresholds
- Logs: Centralized log management and analysis
- Dashboards: Custom visualization of metrics
- Events/EventBridge: React to AWS resource state changes
- Detailed monitoring: 1-minute intervals (vs 5-min default)

questions.question: [AWS Cloud Basics Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: What is the AWS service that provides resizable virtual server instances in the cloud?
questions.explanation: Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) provides secure, resizable compute capacity in the form of virtual servers. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: aws-certified-cloud-practitioner-q1)

questions.question: [AWS Security Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Which AWS security tool allows you to control inbound and outbound traffic at the individual Amazon EC2 instance level?
questions.explanation: Security Groups act as a virtual firewall for your EC2 instances to control incoming and outgoing traffic (stateful). (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: aws-certified-cloud-practitioner-q2)

questions.question: [AWS S3 Storage Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Which AWS storage service is best suited for storing object data like images, videos, and static files?
questions.explanation: Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3) is an object storage service offering industry-leading scalability and data availability. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: aws-certified-cloud-practitioner-q3)

questions.question: [AWS RDS Database Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: A company wants to deploy a relational SQL database in AWS and offload database patching and backups. Which service should they choose?
options: Amazon EC2 with SQL Server installed
questions.explanation: Amazon RDS handles database administration tasks like patching, backups, and OS updates. (Difficulty: Easy, Question Ref: aws-certified-cloud-practitioner-q4)

questions.question: [AWS Content Delivery Scenario 1] In your company's Project A environment: Which AWS service helps users distribute content globally to end-users with low latency by caching content at edge locations?
